en.title: Rork App Performance Optimization — Rendering, Memory & Network Techniques
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Dramatically improve the performance of your Rork-built mobile apps. A practical guide covering rendering optimization, memory management, and network efficiency with code examples.
en.content:
Why Performance Optimization Matters

Now that Rork makes it easy to build mobile apps quickly, the next step is polishing the user experience. Even a one-second delay in app loading can significantly increase user drop-off rates. This article covers concrete techniques for improving Rork app performance across three areas: rendering, memory, and networking.

Rendering Optimization

Preventing Unnecessary Re-renders

In React Native-based Rork apps, component re-rendering is often the biggest performance bottleneck. Using React.memo and useMemo properly eliminates unnecessary redraws.

Tuning FlatList Performance

When displaying large datasets, FlatList configuration is critical.

Memory Management

Image Memory Optimization

Images consume the most memory in mobile apps. In Rork apps, proper caching strategies and size specifications can dramatically reduce memory usage.

Cleanup in useEffect

The leading cause of memory leaks is subscriptions and timers that aren't cleaned up on unmount.

Network Efficiency

Debouncing and Caching Requests

For features like search that trigger frequent requests, combining debounce with caching is highly effective.

Performance Measurement

To verify your optimizations are working, build measurement into your app.

FAQ

Q1. Can I optimize performance through Rork prompts alone?

A1. Basic optimizations like using FlatList and specifying image sizes can be requested through prompts. However, advanced optimizations like memory leak fixes and network caching strategies require code-level adjustments after exporting the project.

Q2. How should I prioritize performance improvements?

A2. Start with initial load speed (the biggest impact on perceived performance), then address scroll jank, and finally tackle memory usage. Use React Native Performance Monitor to identify bottlenecks before diving in.

Q3. Should removeClippedSubviews always be enabled?

A3. It's effective for long lists (50+ items) but can cause bugs with short lists or lists containing complex animations. Only enable it when you've confirmed a measurable performance improvement.

Q4. What image caching library do you recommend?

A4. For React Native, expo-image is the top recommendation. It handles automatic cache management, placeholder display, and BlurHash support out of the box, and works well with Rork apps.

Q5. What's the best approach when API responses are slow?

A5. Combine three techniques: optimistic UI (update the interface before the response arrives), skeleton screens (improve loading perception), and background prefetching (preload the next likely data). Together, these make slow APIs feel fast.

Summary

Rork app performance optimization rests on three pillars: rendering (React.memo / FlatList tuning), memory (image optimization / cleanup), and networking (debounce / caching). You don't need to implement everything at once — start with the highest-impact area based on your measurements.

For related reading, check out Rork Basics Guide and Rork AI Features.

en.title: Triple Your Rork Max App Launch Speed — Performance Optimization Techniques
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Dramatically reduce React Native/Expo app startup time with practical optimization techniques. Learn about code splitting, bundle optimization, memory management, and more—all with real code examples.
en.content:
App launch speed is a critical metric that directly impacts user experience. For mobile applications, those crucial first seconds can determine whether users stay or abandon your app for a competitor. In this article, we'll explore practical optimization techniques that can triple your app's startup performance when building with Rork Max.

Why App Launch Speed Matters

According to Google's User Experience Report, when an app takes more than 3 seconds to launch, user abandonment increases significantly. Mobile users are particularly sensitive to startup performance, making it a key differentiator from competing apps.

Impact of launch speed improvements:

  • User satisfaction +35%
  • Retention rate +20%
  • App Store rating improvements
  • Reduced CPA (Cost Per Acquisition)
  • Better App Store ranking algorithms

1. Bundle Size Optimization

The most effective way to reduce startup time is to minimize bundle size. Expo CLI provides powerful tools for this analysis.

Analyzing Bundle Size

This tool helps identify which libraries are bloating your bundle and where optimization efforts should focus.

Eliminating Unnecessary Dependencies

Smart imports dramatically reduce bundle size:

By importing only the functions you use, you can reduce bundle size by over 90% for that library.

2. Code Splitting and Lazy Loading

Don't load components during app startup if they're not immediately needed. React Native supports dynamic imports for this purpose.

Using React.lazy with Suspense

Lazy Loading with React Navigation

This approach defers loading of non-critical screens until they're actually needed, reducing initial startup time.

3. Memory Leak Prevention and Garbage Collection

Memory leaks gradually degrade app performance. Proper cleanup in useEffect is essential for long-term performance.

Common Memory Leak Patterns and Solutions

Proper EventEmitter Listener Management

4. Leveraging Native Modules

Offloading heavy computations to native code can dramatically improve performance.

Using Expo Modules for Performance-Critical Operations

Executing heavy computations in native code prevents JavaScript thread blocking, keeping the UI responsive.

5. Image Optimization and Caching

Images often comprise a large portion of app bundle size. Optimization here yields significant returns.

Choosing Optimal Image Formats

Embracing WebP Format

WebP provides significantly smaller file sizes than PNG—often 30% reduction—making it ideal for reducing launch time impact.

6. Enabling the Hermes JavaScript Engine

Hermes, developed by Meta, is a JavaScript engine optimized for React Native performance.

Configuring Hermes in app.json

Hermes provides measurable benefits:

  • 20-30% reduction in startup time
  • Lower memory consumption
  • Smaller bundle sizes
  • Improved overall app performance

7. Optimizing Startup Screens

Utilize native splash screens to mask startup time and improve perceived performance.

This approach keeps the splash screen visible during initialization, providing visual feedback to users and masking the actual startup time.

8. Bundle Size Monitoring and Performance Budgets

Continuous monitoring helps detect performance regressions early before they impact users.

Measuring Performance Improvements

Quantifying improvements is essential for validation and continuous optimization.

Real-World Results

By implementing these techniques in combination:

  • Bundle size reduction: 30-40% through dependency optimization
  • Code splitting impact: 20-25% faster initial load
  • Memory management: 15% improvement in stability
  • Native offloading: 10-20% reduction in JavaScript execution
  • Hermes engine: 20-30% JavaScript execution speedup
  • Overall combined effect: 3x faster app launch

Conclusion

Achieving a 3x improvement in app launch speed is realistic when you combine these strategies:

  1. Minimize bundle size - Remove unnecessary dependencies (30-40% reduction)
  2. Implement code splitting - Lazy load non-critical features (20-25% improvement)
  3. Fix memory issues - Prevent leaks and optimize garbage collection (15% gain)
  4. Use native modules - Offload heavy computations (10-20% improvement)
  5. Enable Hermes - Optimize JavaScript execution (20-30% speedup)
  6. Smart splash screens - Improve perceived performance

When applied strategically, these techniques deliver measurable, significant performance improvements that users will immediately notice and appreciate.

en.title: Rork Max Dark Mode & Theming Guide — Customizing Your App's Appearance
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Complete dark mode, light mode, and custom theming implementation. Design token management and modern UI patterns for Rork Max.
en.content:
Dark Mode & Theming in Rork Max

Dark mode has evolved from a nice-to-have feature to an essential requirement. Users expect apps to respect their system preferences, reduce eye strain during evening use, and conserve battery on OLED displays. Creating a themeable app demonstrates attention to user experience and accessibility.

This guide covers implementing light and dark modes, plus building flexible theming systems that let users customize your app's appearance.

Organizing Design Tokens

Color Palette and Design System

The foundation of effective theming is centralizing design tokens—colors, spacing, typography, and other design values.

Centralizing tokens eliminates duplication and makes theme switching effortless.

Managing Global Theme State

Context API for Theme Distribution

React Context allows you to make the current theme accessible throughout your app without prop drilling.

Applying Themes to Components

Using Theme Values in Components

Components consume theme values from the context hook, automatically updating when the theme changes.

This pattern ensures components always use the correct colors for the current theme.

Persisting User Preferences

Saving Theme Selection

Users expect their theme choice to persist across app sessions. Store preferences locally.

Dark Mode Best Practices

Accessibility and Contrast

Sufficient color contrast is critical for readability and accessibility compliance.

WCAG 2.1 guidelines recommend a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Aim for 7:1 when possible.

Building Custom Themes

User-Defined Theme Creation

Enable advanced users to create personalized themes.

Integration with Other Features

Applying consistent theming across camera and gallery screens is covered in Rork Max Camera & Gallery Guide.

For comprehensive design system alignment with design tools, see Rork Max Figma Integration Guide. Learn how to animate theme transitions smoothly in Rork Max Animations Guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: Why does my theme flicker when switching?

A: Re-renders are causing visual jank. Solutions: (1) Memoize components with React.memo, (2) Use useCallback for functions, (3) Cache StyleSheet.create calls. Profile performance with React DevTools to identify bottlenecks.

Q2: Does dark mode implementation differ between iOS and Android?

A: The core approach is identical. useColorScheme handles platform-specific system theme detection automatically, so Rork Max abstracts away implementation details.

Q3: How much effort to add dark mode to an existing app?

A: Design token setup typically takes 2-3 days. The effort scales with component count. Investing time in token design upfront pays dividends in maintainability and consistency across your codebase.

Well-executed theming transforms your app into a flexible, user-focused experience. Invest in solid design tokens and respect user preferences to create apps users genuinely enjoy using.

en.title: Implementing In-App Purchases & Subscriptions in Rork Apps with RevenueCat
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to integrate RevenueCat with Rork to implement in-app purchases and subscriptions. A practical guide to monetizing your Rork app on App Store and Google Play.
en.content:
Why Use RevenueCat with Rork

Building a great app with Rork is only half the journey — generating sustainable revenue is what turns a side project into a real business. In-app purchases and subscriptions are the most powerful monetization tools available to mobile developers today, and RevenueCat is the industry-standard SDK for handling them.

The App Store and Google Play each have their own In-App Purchase (IAP) APIs, and managing both independently is notoriously complex. RevenueCat solves this by providing a unified layer over both platforms, making it an ideal companion for Rork's prompt-driven development workflow.

This guide walks you through integrating RevenueCat into a Rork app from scratch, complete with working code examples and real-world best practices.

What Is RevenueCat?

RevenueCat is a subscription management platform for mobile apps that abstracts away the complexity of native store billing APIs. Since its founding in 2018, it has become the go-to solution for indie developers and startups — tracking billions in annual revenue across its platform.

Key Benefits

  • Cross-platform unified API: Manage App Store and Google Play subscriptions with a single SDK
  • Automatic receipt validation: Server-side fraud protection handled for you
  • Real-time analytics dashboard: Track MRR, churn rate, LTV, and more
  • Webhooks and integrations: Connect to Slack, Amplitude, Mixpanel, and other tools
  • Free tier: No cost until you exceed $2,500/month in tracked revenue

Unlike Stripe — which handles web-based payments — RevenueCat works through Apple and Google's native billing systems, ensuring full compliance with platform guidelines out of the box.

Prerequisites

Before diving in, make sure you have the following ready:

  1. A Rork account (Junior plan or above recommended)
  2. A RevenueCat account (free to create at revenuecat.com)
  3. Apple Developer Program membership ($99/year)
  4. Google Play Developer account ($25 one-time fee)
  5. In-App Purchase products configured in App Store Connect and Google Play Console

Setting Up Products in App Store Connect

  1. Go to App Store Connect → "My Apps" → select your app
  2. Click "In-App Purchases" → tap "+"
  3. Select "Auto-Renewable Subscription"
  4. Enter a product ID (e.g., com.yourapp.premium_monthly) and display name
  5. Set your pricing tier (e.g., $4.99/month)

Repeat the same process in Google Play Console under "Monetize → Subscriptions."

RevenueCat Setup

Step 1: Create a RevenueCat Project

Sign up at RevenueCat and create a new project.

  1. Dashboard → "+ New Project"
  2. Name your project (e.g., MyApp)
  3. Add your iOS and Android apps
  4. Copy your Public SDK Key — you'll need it shortly

Step 2: Configure Entitlements and Offerings

RevenueCat's Entitlements define what content or features a paying user can access.

  • Entitlement example: premium (access to all premium features)
  • Product: attach your App Store / Google Play product IDs here
  • Offering: the set of plans you present to the user at the paywall

Create a premium Entitlement in the dashboard and attach both your iOS and Android product IDs to it.

Implementation in Rork

Step 3: Bootstrap with a Rork Prompt

In your Rork chat, use this prompt to scaffold the RevenueCat integration:

Integrate RevenueCat for subscription management:
- Package: react-native-purchases (RevenueCat official SDK)
- iOS API key: YOUR_IOS_API_KEY
- Android API key: YOUR_ANDROID_API_KEY
- Create a paywall screen showing monthly and annual plans
- Manage purchase state globally using React Context
- Gate premium features based on the user's subscription status

Step 4: Initialize the RevenueCat SDK

Verify that the generated code includes proper SDK initialization:

Step 5: Manage Subscription State Globally

Create a Context to share subscription status across your entire app:

Step 6: Build the Paywall Screen

Step 7: Gate Premium Features

Common Errors and Fixes

Error: "This in-app purchase has already been bought"

Cause: The user is trying to purchase something they already own. Fix: Call Purchases.restorePurchases() to sync their existing subscription.

Error: Sandbox testing fails on device

Cause: No Sandbox tester account is configured in App Store Connect. Fix: Go to App Store Connect → "Users and Access" → "Sandbox Testers", create a test account, and sign in on your physical device before testing.

Error: Google Play product shows "not available"

Cause: The product hasn't been activated, or the app hasn't been published to at least the internal testing track. Fix: Set the product status to "Active" in Google Play Console and publish your app to the internal test track before attempting purchases.

Error: Purchases.configure() called multiple times

Cause: The initialization runs on every component re-render. Fix: Ensure useEffect has an empty dependency array [] so it runs only once at app startup.

Advanced: Webhooks and Backend Sync

RevenueCat's Webhook feature lets you receive real-time events — new purchase, renewal, cancellation, trial start — and react to them in your backend.

Practical Use Cases

  • Slack alerts: Notify your team channel every time a new subscription starts
  • Analytics pipelines: Push revenue events to Amplitude or Mixpanel
  • Database sync: Keep your Supabase or Firebase user records in sync with subscription status

Here's an example Supabase Edge Function that receives RevenueCat webhooks and updates your database:

FAQ

Q: Is RevenueCat free to use?

A: Yes — RevenueCat is free as long as your app's monthly tracked revenue stays under $2,500. Beyond that, a percentage-based fee applies, but the time savings on cross-platform billing management far outweigh the cost.

Q: Does RevenueCat work with Rork Max native Swift apps?

A: Absolutely. RevenueCat provides a native Swift SDK for iOS. When building with Rork Max, prompt Claude Opus 4.6 to integrate the RevenueCat iOS SDK and it will generate the appropriate SwiftUI code, including StoreKit configuration and paywall screens.

Q: Can I manage iOS and Android subscriptions in one place?

A: That's exactly what RevenueCat was built for. One project, one dashboard — you can see all subscribers, revenue metrics, and entitlement status across both platforms in a unified view.

Q: How do I implement a free trial period?

A: Configure a free trial (e.g., 7 days) directly in App Store Connect or Google Play Console on the subscription product. RevenueCat automatically handles the trial state — no extra code needed. You can detect trial status via customerInfo.entitlements.active['premium']?.periodType === 'trial'.

Q: What happens if a user reinstalls the app?

A: Call Purchases.restorePurchases() to restore their previous purchases tied to their App Store or Google Play account. Always include a "Restore Purchases" button in your paywall — it's required by the App Store Review Guidelines.

Conclusion

RevenueCat and Rork together form an incredibly efficient stack for building and monetizing mobile apps. With RevenueCat handling the complexities of cross-platform billing, you can focus entirely on building features your users love.

Here's a quick recap of the key integration points:

  • Configure Entitlements, Products, and Offerings in the RevenueCat dashboard before writing code
  • Initialize the SDK exactly once at app launch with Purchases.configure()
  • Share subscription state globally with a PurchaseContext
  • Always include a "Restore Purchases" button — it's required by App Store guidelines
  • Use Webhooks to keep your backend in sync with subscription events automatically

Ready to take your monetization strategy further? Check out App Business Strategy for Solo Developers for growth tactics, and Integrating Stripe Payments into Rork Apps if you also need web-based payment flows. For pricing guidance on getting started, see Rork Pricing Plans Explained.

en.title: Rork Max Social Login Guide — Adding Google, Apple & GitHub Authentication
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Complete guide to implementing Google, Apple, and GitHub authentication in Rork Max. OAuth2 flows, token management, and session handling.
en.content:
Social Authentication in Rork Max

Users increasingly expect frictionless authentication experiences. Asking them to create yet another password-protected account creates barriers to adoption. Social login eliminates this friction by letting users sign in with existing Google, Apple, or GitHub accounts—dramatically improving both registration completion rates and user satisfaction.

This guide walks you through implementing OAuth 2.0-based authentication with major social providers in Rork Max applications.

Understanding OAuth 2.0

How Social Authentication Works

OAuth 2.0 enables users to grant your app access to their resources without sharing passwords with you. This standard protocol underpins modern authentication.

The basic flow:

  1. User clicks "Sign in with Google"
  2. User is redirected to Google's authorization screen
  3. User grants permission
  4. Your app receives an authorization code
  5. Your backend exchanges the code for a token
  6. User session is created

This approach protects user credentials while enabling seamless authentication.

Implementing Google Sign-In

Google Sign-In Integration

Google login is the most widely adopted social login method worldwide.

Implementing Apple Sign-In

Native iOS Sign in with Apple

iOS 13+ made Sign in with Apple available, and Apple now encourages its adoption in App Store apps.

Implementing GitHub Login

GitHub Authentication for Developer Apps

For developer-oriented applications, GitHub authentication is a natural choice.

Token Management and Session Persistence

Secure Token Storage

Tokens should never be stored in localStorage. Use secure storage mechanisms instead.

Integrating with Profile Features

Profile images from social logins can be displayed alongside custom uploads. See Rork Max Camera & Gallery Guide for file management techniques.

Post-login navigation and animations are covered in Rork Max Animations Guide. For a complete authentication solution using Firebase or Supabase, refer to Rork Max Firebase & Supabase Authentication Guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: How should I handle the same email address across multiple social providers?

A: When a user signs in with a different provider using the same email, offer to link the accounts to their existing profile, create a new profile, or let them choose. Implementing email verification ensures you're connecting the right accounts.

Q2: Can users transition from social login to password-based authentication?

A: Yes—implement a password setup flow allowing users to set their own credentials. Afterward, support both traditional email+password login and social login for maximum flexibility.

Q3: How do I request additional scopes after initial login?

A: Use incremental authorization. Request minimal scopes initially, then prompt for additional permissions later when needed. Google and Apple both support re-triggering auth flows to add new scopes to existing tokens.

Social login removes friction from user onboarding while maintaining security. When implemented thoughtfully, it dramatically improves conversion rates and user satisfaction. Balance convenience with security to build trust.

en.title: Rork Max × AI Image Generation — Integrating AI Image Creation into Your App
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Master DALL-E, Imagen, and other AI image APIs in Rork Max. Learn prompt engineering, caching, and error handling strategies.
en.content:
AI Image Generation in Rork Max Apps

The ability to generate stunning images from text descriptions represents a fundamental shift in creative app development. When integrated into mobile applications, these capabilities unlock new possibilities: design assistants, creative tools, personalized content generation, and much more.

This guide walks you through implementing DALL-E, Imagen, and other leading AI image generation APIs in Rork Max applications.

Understanding AI Image Generation

The Power of Prompt Engineering

Creating high-quality images depends heavily on well-crafted prompts. Prompt engineering is the art of directing AI models toward your desired outcomes.

Effective prompts typically include:

  • Subject: What to generate
  • Style: Artistic direction (e.g., watercolor, photography, 3D render)
  • Quality indicators: High resolution, detailed, cinematic
  • Setting: Environment or context

Example: "A serene Japanese garden with cherry blossoms, watercolor painting style, highly detailed, 8k resolution"

Implementing DALL-E Integration

OpenAI DALL-E 3 API

DALL-E remains one of the most capable image generation APIs available, known for its versatility and quality.

DALL-E provides exceptional quality but incurs per-request costs. Implementing caching strategies is essential for cost management.

Google Imagen Integration

Leveraging Vertex AI Imagen

Google Cloud's Imagen API delivers quality comparable to DALL-E while offering enterprise-grade features and compliance options.

Mastering Prompt Optimization

Designing Effective Prompts

Prompt quality directly impacts output quality. Well-structured prompts leverage AI models' full capabilities.

Using prompt templates ensures consistency while dramatically improving output quality across multiple generations.

Caching and Performance

Implementing Smart Image Caching

Avoid regenerating identical images by implementing intelligent caching. This reduces costs and improves response times.

Cache size is managed using an LRU (Least Recently Used) algorithm to maintain memory efficiency.

Error Handling and Rate Limiting

Robust API Management

AI APIs enforce rate limits. Implementing proper error handling and retry logic is critical.

Real-World Applications

Combine image generation with camera capabilities for powerful creative tools. Learn more in Rork Max Camera & Gallery Guide.

Integrate with authentication to offer personalized image generation experiences. See Rork Max Social Login Implementation Guide for details.

Add visual polish with animations. Check Rork Max Animations Guide for techniques.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: How can I maximize image generation quality?

A: Include specific details in your prompts. Instead of "landscape," use "Kyoto temple at golden hour with cherry blossoms, oil painting style, 8k resolution." Also select quality options (DALL-E's "hd" setting) when available. Iterate and refine based on results.

Q2: How can I speed up generation time?

A: APIs typically complete in 30 seconds to 2 minutes. Aggressively cache results to avoid regenerating identical prompts. Use asynchronous processing so users can interact with your app while generation happens in the background.

Q3: What are the copyright and licensing considerations?

A: Generated images typically belong to the user under the API service's terms. For commercial applications, review each service's licensing agreement carefully. Additionally, clearly specify image ownership rights in your app's terms of service.

Thoughtfully implemented AI image generation elevates your app from functional to exceptional. Focus on prompt quality, intelligent caching, and robust error handling to create experiences users will love.

en.title: Rork Max Maps & Location Guide — Implementing MapView and Geolocation
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Complete guide to map display, geolocation tracking, markers, and directions in Rork Max. Ideal for rideshare and location-based apps.
en.content:
Maps & Location Features in Rork Max

Location-based functionality has become central to modern mobile applications. From ride-sharing platforms and restaurant discovery to fitness tracking and delivery management, geolocation capabilities enable rich user experiences that were unimaginable just a decade ago.

This comprehensive guide covers implementing maps, geolocation tracking, markers, and route navigation in Rork Max applications.

Setting Up Basic Map Display

MapView Fundamentals

Rork Max leverages react-native-maps to provide cross-platform map functionality. Proper initialization with API keys is essential for full functionality.

This creates an interactive map centered on Tokyo with a single marker. The onRegionChange callback allows you to respond to user pan and zoom gestures.

Implementing Geolocation

Real-Time Location Tracking

The Geolocation API provides access to the device's GPS for continuous position updates.

Setting enableHighAccuracy to true provides better precision at the cost of increased battery drain. Adjust based on your app's requirements.

Working with Markers

Displaying Multiple Points of Interest

Markers effectively visualize multiple locations on your map. Customizing their appearance helps users distinguish between different location types.

The color-coded markers provide instant visual feedback about location categories, improving usability.

Route Navigation and Directions

Computing Routes Between Points

Integrating the Google Maps Directions API enables turn-by-turn navigation and distance/duration calculations.

Managing Location Permissions

Handling Privacy-First Permissions

Modern platforms enforce granular permission controls. iOS 13+ offers three levels: "Always," "While Using," and "Never."

Transparency about location usage builds user trust. Always explain why your app needs this sensitive permission.

Integration with Other Features

Location data enriches many app features. For fitness tracking visualization, check out Rork Max Animations Guide.

User authentication for location-based services is covered in Rork Max Social Login Implementation Guide. For foundational architecture knowledge, see React Native & Expo Architecture Guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: How can I reduce battery drain while tracking location?

A: Adjust the maximumAge option to cache recent positions (e.g., 10 seconds). This reduces unnecessary GPS queries. Also call clearWatch when users navigate away from the tracking screen.

Q2: Can maps work offline in Rork Max?

A: Offline map display requires pre-downloading tiles using libraries like react-native-maps-offline. Sync cached tiles when connectivity returns for current data.

Q3: How do I optimize performance with many markers?

A: Implement marker clustering for 100+ markers. Libraries like react-native-maps-clustering group nearby markers automatically, dramatically improving performance and user experience.

Well-implemented maps and location features transform your app into a powerful location-aware platform. Focus on smooth permissions, responsive interactions, and user privacy to build trust and engagement.

en.title: Rork Max AI Features Guide — On-Device ML & Cloud AI Integration
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Add AI capabilities to Rork Max apps. Covers on-device inference with TensorFlow Lite/Core ML, cloud API integration (OpenAI/Gemini), image recognition, NLP, and recommendation systems.
en.content:
Rork Max AI Features Guide

Integrating AI into Rork Max apps transforms user experience and creates competitive advantages. This guide explores two complementary approaches: on-device machine learning (TensorFlow Lite) and cloud-based AI APIs (OpenAI, Gemini), with practical implementation patterns for each.

On-Device ML vs Cloud AI Decision Matrix

| Factor | On-Device ML | Cloud AI API | |--------|--------------|--------------| | Latency | Low (<100ms) | Higher (300ms+) | | Privacy | ✓ Local only | ⚠️ Server transmission | | Bundle Size | Large (10–100MB) | Small | | Accuracy | Limited | High | | Updates | Manual download | Automatic | | Cost | Development | Per-API-call | | Connectivity | Not required | Required | | Model Control | Full | Limited |

ℹ️
**Hybrid Strategy**: Use on-device ML for privacy-sensitive, low-latency tasks (face detection, text analysis). Use cloud APIs for high-accuracy requirements (natural language understanding, content generation). Combine both for optimal UX.

Part 1: On-Device ML with TensorFlow Lite

Part 3: OpenAI API Integration

GPT-4o Chat Integration

Recommendation Engine with Collaborative Filtering

Summary

On-Device ML Strengths

  • ✓ Privacy-first
  • ✓ Offline capability
  • ✓ Low latency

  • ✗ Limited accuracy

Cloud AI Strengths

  • ✓ High accuracy
  • ✓ Continuous improvement
  • ✓ Smaller app size
  • ✗ Requires internet
  • ✗ API costs

Best Practice: Combine both. Use on-device ML for fast, privacy-critical tasks. Use cloud APIs for high-accuracy, complex reasoning. This hybrid approach delivers the best user experience.

en.title: Rork Max Camera & Gallery Guide — Implementing Photo Capture and Image Selection
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Complete guide to implementing camera access and gallery features in Rork Max apps. From native API integration to image optimization.
en.content:
Camera & Gallery Features in Rork Max

Photography and image selection capabilities have become essential for modern mobile applications. Whether you're building a social networking app, e-commerce platform, or document management system, providing seamless camera and gallery integration directly impacts user engagement and satisfaction.

This guide walks you through implementing robust camera and gallery features in Rork Max, covering everything from permission management to image optimization techniques.

Getting Started with Camera Integration

Understanding Permission Requirements

Before accessing the device camera, you must request the appropriate permissions. On iOS, these are declared in Info.plist, while on Android, they're specified in AndroidManifest.xml. Rork Max simplifies this process with built-in permission handling.

This code handles the permission request lifecycle gracefully, providing user feedback while maintaining clear state management.

Implementing Image Selection

Building a Gallery Picker

The image picker functionality allows users to select photos from their device library. Rork Max integrates seamlessly with platform-specific image picker libraries.

The configuration options allow you to control quality, maximum dimensions, and media type, ensuring consistency across different devices.

Image Processing and Optimization

Resizing and Compression

High-resolution images directly from the camera consume significant bandwidth and storage. Implementing image optimization is critical for app performance and user experience.

This approach typically reduces file size by 50-70% while maintaining acceptable visual quality for most use cases.

Enhancing User Experience

Progress Tracking and Error Handling

When uploading images, especially larger files, showing progress updates keeps users informed and improves perceived performance.

Performance Best Practices

Memory Management Strategies

Camera operations are memory-intensive. Implementing these practices prevents crashes and ensures smooth operation:

  • Image Caching: Avoid redundant reloading of the same images
  • Proper Cleanup: Release resources when components unmount
  • Background Processing: Offload heavy image operations to worker threads

For more on native performance optimization, see the Rork Max Native API Guide.

Integration with Other Features

User profile images often integrate with authentication systems. Learn more in the Rork Max Social Login Implementation Guide.

For adding polish to your image UI with animations, check out the Rork Max Animations Guide.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: Is there a functional difference between camera-captured and gallery photos?

A: The core processing is identical, but camera photos may include EXIF metadata such as geolocation and capture timestamp. For privacy reasons, consider stripping EXIF data before uploading user images.

Q2: How can I allow users to select multiple images at once?

A: Use the selectionLimit option in react-native-image-picker. Setting selectionLimit: 0 enables unlimited selection. However, UX best practices suggest capping this at around 10 images to prevent performance issues.

Q3: Can I restrict file types to only PNG and JPEG?

A: After receiving the picker response, check response.assets[0].type to validate the file format. Display an error message if unsupported formats are selected, and guide users to choose compatible formats.

Implementing camera and gallery features thoughtfully elevates your app's functionality. Focus on smooth permission handling, efficient image optimization, and clear user feedback to create a professional experience that users will appreciate.

Summary

Implementing i18n in Rork Max requires careful planning but pays off with global reach:

  • File Organization: Structure translations by namespace and locale

  • RTL Support: Automatically adapt layout for right-to-left languages

With these foundations, you can confidently build apps for a truly global audience.

en.title: Rork Max Security Best Practices — API Key Protection, Auth & Data Encryption
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Harden security in Rork Max apps. Covers API key management, token storage (expo-secure-store), transport encryption, data protection, and App Store compliance.
en.content:
Rork Max Security Best Practices

Security breaches destroy user trust instantly. This guide walks through proven patterns to eliminate vulnerabilities, from API key management to data encryption, with production-ready code examples.

The Three Pillars of Mobile App Security

  1. Transport Security: HTTPS and certificate pinning
  2. Authentication & Authorization: Token-based auth with secure storage
  3. Data Protection: Encryption at rest and in transit

Part 1: API Key Management — The Golden Rule

❌ Never Do This

✓ The Correct Pattern: Backend Proxy

The mobile client never holds API keys. All requests route through your backend.

[Rork Max App] → [Your Backend] → [OpenAI/Gemini API]
  (Auth Token)    (API Key)      (Secured)

src/services/apiClient.ts

Backend Implementation (Node.js/Express)

⚠️
**Critical Rule**: Never include API keys, database credentials, or secrets in client code or environment files that get bundled. Use a backend proxy exclusively.

Part 2: Secure Token Storage

Use expo-secure-store to encrypt tokens at rest.

Installation

Auth Manager

src/services/authManager.ts

Login Implementation

src/screens/LoginScreen.tsx

Part 3: Biometric Authentication

Passphrase-less auth improves both UX and security.

Installation

src/services/biometricAuth.ts

Integrate with Login

Part 4: HTTPS & Certificate Pinning

Every request must use HTTPS. Consider certificate pinning for sensitive backends.

src/services/httpClient.ts

Timeout Configuration

Part 5: Data Protection at Rest

Separate storage strategies by sensitivity.

src/services/storage.ts

Storage Decision Matrix

| Data | Storage | Reason | |------|---------|--------| | API keys | ✗ Never | Backend only | | Access tokens | SecureStore | Encrypted | | User ID | AsyncStorage | Non-sensitive | | Theme preference | AsyncStorage | Non-sensitive | | Payment info | ✗ Never | PCI compliance | | Health data | SecureStore | Sensitive |

Part 6: OWASP Mobile Security Top 10

1. Insecure Data Storage

2. Insecure Communication

3. Insecure Authentication

4. Insufficient Cryptography

5. Reverse Engineering

Security Checklist

Code

  • [ ] No hardcoded secrets
  • [ ] Tokens in SecureStore only
  • [ ] All network requests use HTTPS
  • [ ] Sensitive logs stripped in production
  • [ ] Error messages don't reveal internals

Authentication

  • [ ] Token refresh before expiry
  • [ ] Tokens cleared on logout
  • [ ] Access token lifetime < 1 hour
  • [ ] Refresh token rotation considered
  • [ ] Biometric auth for convenience

Network

  • [ ] HTTPS enforced
  • [ ] Certificate validation enabled
  • [ ] Request timeouts set (30s max)
  • [ ] No plaintext credentials in requests

Data

  • [ ] Only necessary data collected
  • [ ] PII encrypted at rest
  • [ ] Old data deleted on logout
  • [ ] Backups excluded from encryption
  • [ ] GDPR/CCPA compliant

App Store Submission

  • [ ] Privacy policy present
  • [ ] Data usage declared
  • [ ] No malware/jailbreak detection bypass
  • [ ] Test credentials used (not real accounts)

Example: Complete Login Flow

Summary

Secure Rork Max apps require:

  1. No Secrets in Code: API keys go to backend only
  2. Encrypted Storage: SecureStore for tokens and PII
  3. HTTPS Always: No HTTP connections
  4. Token Rotation: Short-lived access tokens
  5. Biometric Auth: Convenience + security
  6. Clear on Logout: Delete all sensitive data
  7. Monitor & Update: Security patches matter

Follow these patterns and your app will pass security audits and earn user trust.

en.title: Rork Max State Management Guide — Zustand, Context API & Redux Toolkit
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Optimize state management in Rork Max apps. Comparison of Zustand, React Context, and Redux Toolkit with implementation examples, performance optimization, and persistence (AsyncStorage/MMKV).
en.content:
Rork Max State Management Guide

As Rork Max apps grow in complexity, choosing the right state management solution becomes critical. This guide compares three leading approaches—Zustand, Context API, and Redux Toolkit—with practical examples, performance tips, and decision-making frameworks.

Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Context API | Zustand | Redux Toolkit | |---------|-------------|---------|---------------| | Learning Curve | Low | Low | Medium–High | | Bundle Size | 0 KB | 3 KB | 15 KB | | Performance | ⚠️ Careful | ✓ Optimized | ✓ Optimized | | Complex State | △ Small–Medium | ✓ Recommended | ✓ Recommended | | Boilerplate | Minimal | Minimal | Significant | | DevTools | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | Time-Travel Debugging | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | | Async Handling | Manual | Manual | Redux Thunk | | TypeScript Support | ✓ | ✓ Excellent | ✓ |

ℹ️
**Quick Decision Guide**: - **Small apps (<10 screens)**: Context API - **Medium to large apps**: Zustand - **Large apps with complex async logic**: Redux Toolkit

Zustand — Minimal & Powerful

Zustand provides powerful state management with minimal boilerplate and excellent TypeScript support.

Installation

Basic Store

src/store/userStore.ts

Using in Components

src/screens/Profile.tsx

Performance Optimization — Selectors Matter

⚠️
**Critical**: Always use selector functions. Without them, the component re-renders whenever any state changes, regardless of relevance.

Combining Multiple Stores

React Context API — Built-in Solution

For small to medium apps, Context API is sufficient and requires no external dependencies.

src/contexts/ThemeContext.tsx

src/App.tsx

Solving Context API Performance Issues

Redux Toolkit — Enterprise-Grade

For large, complex apps with sophisticated async workflows, Redux Toolkit provides structure and powerful debugging tools.

Installation

Create a Slice

src/store/slices/authSlice.ts

Configure Store

src/store/index.ts

src/App.tsx

Use in Components

src/screens/Login.tsx

Persistence — AsyncStorage vs MMKV

Persist state across app launches for seamless session recovery.

Zustand + AsyncStorage

Use MMKV for Performance

ℹ️
**MMKV Benefits**: 100x faster than AsyncStorage. Ideal for high-frequency writes and large datasets. Use it for frequently updated state like user preferences and cache data.

Decision Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Choice | Reason | |----------|------------|--------| | Simple theme toggle | Context API | Zero dependencies | | Global user state | Zustand | Minimal setup | | Shopping cart | Zustand or Redux | Persists across sessions | | Complex multi-step form | Zustand | Flexible actions | | Real-time notifications | Redux | Thunk async handling | | Enterprise app | Redux | DevTools, structure |

Best Practices

1. Separate State Logic

2. Avoid Over-Nesting

3. Normalize Data

Summary

  • Context API: Perfect for small apps, no setup overhead
  • Zustand: Sweet spot for most apps—minimal boilerplate, great TypeScript
  • Redux: Enterprise solutions with complex workflows and debugging needs

Choose based on app complexity, team expertise, and debugging requirements.

en.title: Rork Max UI Design Tips — 10 Rules for Beautiful Apps
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: 10 actionable rules for professional UI design in Rork Max apps. Color palette, typography, spacing, animations, dark mode, and practical techniques you can use immediately.
en.content:
Rork Max UI Design Tips — 10 Rules for Beautiful Apps

Great UI design isn't magic—it's following proven principles consistently. This guide shares 10 actionable rules used by professional designers, with code examples you can apply immediately.

Rule 1: The 60-30-10 Color Principle

Professional UIs follow a proven color ratio that creates harmony and visual hierarchy.

  • 60%: Primary color (background, main brand)
  • 30%: Secondary color (UI elements, cards, text)
  • 10%: Accent color (buttons, highlights, CTAs)

src/styles/colors.ts

src/screens/Home.tsx

ℹ️
**Pro Tip**: Test your color palette with colorblind-friendly tools. Websites like ColorOracle simulate different types of color blindness to ensure your design is accessible.

Rule 2: Typography Hierarchy

Clear size progression makes content scannable and establishes visual hierarchy.

src/styles/typography.ts

Usage Example

⚠️
**Best Practice**: Limit to 1–2 font families maximum. Use system fonts (SF Pro Display on iOS, Roboto on Android) for optimal rendering and performance.

Rule 3: Spacing & Padding Consistency

Use an 8px grid system. Consistency creates rhythm and balance.

src/styles/spacing.ts

| Component | Padding | Margin Bottom | |-----------|---------|---------------| | Card | 16px | 12px | | Button | 12px V, 16px H | 8px | | List Item | 16px | 8px | | Section | 24px | 24px |

Rule 4: Minimum Touch Target — 44×44px

Apple and Google both recommend minimum 44×44pt touch targets for accessibility.

src/components/Button.tsx

Rule 5: Purposeful Animation

Animation should inform, not distract. Every animation communicates state change.

src/components/FadeInView.tsx

Scale Animation on Press

ℹ️
**Animation Timing**: - Page transitions: 200–300ms - Icon interactions: 200ms - Button feedback: 100–150ms - Always use `useNativeDriver={true}` for performance

Rule 6: Dark Mode Support

Dark mode is standard on iOS and Android. Detect and adapt automatically.

src/hooks/useTheme.ts

src/screens/Profile.tsx

Navigation Theme

Rule 7: Icon Consistency

Pick one icon library and stick with it. Material Icons or SF Symbols work well.

src/components/Icon.tsx

Rule 8: State Representations

Every screen needs three states: Loading, Empty, and Error.

src/components/StateViews.tsx

Rule 9: List Performance with FlatList

Never render 1000 items at once. Use FlatList with optimizations.

Rule 10: Accessibility

Design for everyone. Screen readers, color blindness, and motor difficulties matter.

src/components/AccessibleButton.tsx

Text Contrast Checker

Design Checklist

  • [ ] Colors: 60-30-10 ratio implemented
  • [ ] Typography: 4–5 clear sizes with hierarchy
  • [ ] Spacing: 8px grid system throughout
  • [ ] Touch targets: All interactive elements 44×44px
  • [ ] Animation: useNativeDriver={true}, timing <300ms
  • [ ] Dark mode: Tested and working
  • [ ] Icons: One library, consistent sizing
  • [ ] States: Loading, Empty, and Error views
  • [ ] Performance: FlatList for large lists
  • [ ] Accessibility: Labels, roles, and 4.5:1 contrast

Summary

Professional UI design is about consistency, not complexity. Follow these 10 rules and your Rork Max app will look and feel like an App Store feature.

en.title: Building Stripe Subscriptions with Rork Max — No-Code In-App Billing at Scale
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: A complete guide to implementing Stripe subscriptions in Rork Max. Build production-ready in-app billing without complex backend code—from customer setup to renewal and cancellation.
en.content:
Introduction

Building a SaaS application or subscription-based service requires robust payment processing and subscription management. However, the complexity of implementing these features often consumes significant development time, pulling attention away from core product work.

Rork Max solves this problem. By integrating with Stripe, developers can build production-ready subscription systems in hours rather than weeks—without wrestling with complex backend logic or API integrations.

This guide walks you through implementing a practical, scalable subscription system using Rork Max and Stripe. Whether you're launching a premium tier, tiered pricing model, or entirely subscription-based product, you'll learn the exact steps to get from zero to production.

Prerequisites

Before diving into this guide, make sure you have:

  • A Rork Max account with basic platform familiarity. If you're new, start with our Stripe payment integration guide.
  • An active Stripe account with API access. You'll need your publishable and secret keys.
  • Comfort with REST APIs and JSON data structures.
  • A database to store customer and subscription data. We'll cover Supabase and Firebase options in the backend guide.

Setting Up Stripe

Before building anything in Rork Max, you need to configure your products and pricing in Stripe.

1. Create Products and Plans

Start by defining what you're selling.

  1. Log into your Stripe dashboard
  2. Navigate to Products under Catalog
  3. Click Add product
  4. Enter a product name (e.g., "Pro Plan") and description
  5. Click Add price and configure:
    • Price: e.g., $99/month
    • Billing period: Monthly
    • Recurring: Yes

Create multiple plans (Starter, Pro, Enterprise) by repeating this process.

2. Get Your API Keys

Rork Max needs your Stripe API credentials.

  1. Go to Developers → API Keys
  2. Copy your Publishable key and Secret key
  3. Note the Webhook signing secret location—you'll need it later

Configuring Rork Max

1. Connect Stripe

Link your Stripe account to Rork Max.

  1. Open your Rork Max dashboard
  2. Navigate to Integrations → Payment Services
  3. Select Stripe and click Connect
  4. Paste your Stripe secret key
  5. Click Confirm

2. Create a Subscriptions Table

Store subscription data in your database.

3. Build Webhook Handler

Stripe sends real-time events (payment success, subscription updates, cancellations). Your webhook endpoint listens for these.

Register the webhook endpoint in Stripe:

  1. Go to Developers → Webhooks
  2. Click Add an endpoint
  3. Enter your Rork Max endpoint URL (e.g., https://yourapp.rorklab.app/webhooks/stripe)
  4. Select events to listen for:
    • customer.subscription.updated
    • customer.subscription.deleted
    • invoice.payment_succeeded
    • invoice.payment_failed
  5. Click Add endpoint

Implementing Subscription Features

1. Creating Subscriptions

Implement the flow for users to start a subscription.

2. Changing and Canceling Plans

Let users upgrade, downgrade, or cancel their subscriptions.

3. Subscription Management Dashboard

Build a customer-facing dashboard where users view and manage their subscription.

Using Rork Max's canvas editor, add:

  • Current plan display: Plan name and monthly cost
  • Billing cycle: Next billing date (current_period_end)
  • Change plan button: Upgrade or downgrade
  • Payment method management: Update card details
  • Cancel button: With confirmation dialog
  • Billing history: Invoice list and download links

Troubleshooting

Common Issues

Webhooks aren't being received

  • Verify the webhook URL is correctly registered in Stripe
  • Check the Events log in Stripe's Webhooks section for errors
  • Confirm your webhook secret is properly set in your environment variables

Payment declined when creating subscription

  • Ensure the payment method is valid (use Stripe test cards in development)
  • Check that customer billing details are complete
  • Review Stripe's fraud detection settings—legitimate payments may be blocked

Proration not calculating when changing plans

  • Verify proration_behavior is set to create_prorations
  • Check that proration is enabled in your Stripe billing settings

FAQ

Q1: Are there additional licensing costs to implement subscriptions in Rork Max?

A: Rork Max itself has no additional fees. You pay Stripe's standard processing fee (typically 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction for card payments). This is the standard cost of processing online payments.

Q2: How do I support multiple currencies (USD, EUR, JPY, etc.)?

A: Stripe supports multi-currency pricing. Create price variants for each currency in your products. In Rork Max, detect the user's location or currency preference and display the appropriate pricing option.

Q3: What's the process for refunding customers or handling subscription disputes?

A: Stripe provides refund functionality through the dashboard or API. Create a Rork Max function that calls the Stripe refund endpoint. Define a clear refund policy first—see our solo dev guide for business best practices.

Q4: Can I test subscriptions before going live?

A: Yes. Stripe provides test mode with test card numbers. Use pk_test_... and sk_test_... keys for development, then switch to live keys (pk_live_... and sk_live_...) for production. Always test the full flow: subscription creation, renewal, cancellation, and refunds.

Conclusion

Combining Rork Max with Stripe lets you build complex subscription systems without the complexity. Following this guide gives you:

  • Secure subscription infrastructure
  • Real-time event handling via webhooks
  • Scalable customer management
  • Proper error handling and edge cases

Subscription business models create stable, predictable revenue and deeper customer relationships. Use Rork Max to focus on what matters—delivering value to users.

Ready to launch? Start with a single plan, test thoroughly with Stripe's test mode, and scale from there. Your users (and your revenue) will thank you.

en.title: Rork Max × Cloudflare Workers AI Backend — AI Gateway, Workers AI & D1 Integration
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Build a production AI backend for Rork Max apps using Cloudflare Workers. Covers AI Gateway for LLM cost reduction, Workers AI for edge inference, D1 for user data, and secure connection patterns from Rork Max.
en.content:
Building a mobile app with Rork Max gives you powerful UI creation capabilities, but adding intelligent AI features requires a scalable, low-latency backend. This guide walks through constructing a production-ready AI backend using Cloudflare Workers, AI Gateway, Workers AI, and D1 SQLite—all without managing servers.

Architecture Overview

Understanding the request flow from Rork Max apps to Cloudflare infrastructure is essential.

Rork Max App (React Native)
        ↓
    HTTPS Request
        ↓
Cloudflare Workers
        ↓
    ┌─────────────────────────┐
    │ AI Gateway              │ ← Cost reduction, caching, observability
    │ ↓                       │
    │ OpenAI / Anthropic API  │
    │ or                      │
    │ Workers AI (Llama etc)  │
    └─────────────────────────┘
        ↓
    D1 SQLite
   (User data, chat history)

Why Cloudflare Workers for AI backends:

  • Edge Computing: Global network delivers responses with minimal latency
  • Serverless Scale: Auto-scales from zero to millions of requests
  • AI Gateway: Caches LLM API calls, reduces costs, provides observability
  • Workers AI: Run inference at the edge (Llama, Mistral), sub-100ms responses
  • D1 Native: SQLite database built-in for user data and conversation history

Cloudflare Workers Setup

Initialize a new Workers project with TypeScript:

Edit wrangler.toml to configure AI Gateway and D1 bindings:

Create the D1 database:

Copy the database_id from the output into wrangler.toml.

TypeScript Types

Define interfaces for type safety across your backend:

AI Gateway Configuration

Log into the Cloudflare dashboard and navigate to AI → AI Gateway. Create a new gateway:

Gateway Name: rork-ai-gateway
Provider: OpenAI (or Anthropic)

AI Gateway provides:

  • Request Caching: Cache identical prompts, eliminate duplicate API calls
  • Multi-Provider Routing: Route requests to different LLM providers based on cost/latency
  • Observability: Log all requests, track token usage, identify bottlenecks
  • Rate Limiting: Protect against abuse, enforce per-user/per-app quotas

Workers AI Integration

Run Llama 2, Mistral 7B, and other models directly at the edge—no external API calls:

Available models: @cf/meta/llama-2-7b-chat-int8, @cf/mistral/mistral-7b-instruct-v0.1, and many others.

D1 Database Schema

Define tables for user profiles, conversations, and message history:

Apply migrations:

JWT Authentication Middleware

Secure all requests from Rork Max with JWT tokens:

RESTful API Endpoints

Design clean API endpoints that Rork Max can call via standard fetch:

Connecting from Rork Max

Call the Workers API from your Rork-generated React Native app:

ℹ️
**Token Management**: On app startup, fetch a JWT token from your backend auth endpoint and store it in AsyncStorage. Include this token in the Authorization header for all subsequent API calls.

Streaming Responses

Enable real-time text streaming for a better user experience:

Consume streaming responses in Rork with Server-Sent Events:

Rate Limiting & DDoS Protection

Add built-in rate limiting to Workers:

Further strengthen protection in the Cloudflare dashboard under Security → DDoS Protection.

Cost Analysis

Workers AI vs OpenAI API:

  • Workers AI: $0.05 per 1M inference tokens
  • OpenAI GPT-3.5: $0.5 per 1M tokens (10× more expensive)

However, Workers AI has massive latency advantages and caches well, offsetting raw per-token costs.

AI Gateway Caching: If 50% of your requests hit the cache, you cut API costs in half. For typical chat apps, this saves thousands per month.

D1 Cost: First 5GB free, then $0.75/GB. 500M monthly messages consume ~10MB—well within free tier.

⚠️
**API Key Management**: Never hardcode API keys. Always store them in Cloudflare Secrets and reference via `env.OPENAI_API_KEY`, etc.

Deployment Checklist

  • [ ] All environment variables set in wrangler.toml
  • [ ] JWT_SECRET rotated to a secure key
  • [ ] D1 migrations applied to production
  • [ ] CORS origins limited to your actual domain
  • [ ] OpenAI/Anthropic keys stored in Cloudflare Secrets
  • [ ] Local testing passed (wrangler dev)
  • [ ] Deployed with wrangler publish
  • [ ] Worker URL configured in Rork Max app
  • [ ] Cloudflare Analytics enabled for monitoring

Summary

Cloudflare Workers provides an ideal foundation for AI-powered Rork Max apps. AI Gateway reduces LLM costs through intelligent caching, Workers AI delivers sub-100ms inference at the edge, and D1 SQLite keeps user data close and queryable. This architecture is production-ready, requires zero server management, and scales globally.

Next steps: implement multi-user authentication, add function calling for tool use, support file uploads for vision models, and track detailed usage metrics to optimize costs further.

en.title: Rork Max Accessibility Implementation Guide — VoiceOver, TalkBack & Dynamic Text
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Complete accessibility implementation guide for Rork Max apps. Covers iOS VoiceOver and Android TalkBack screen readers, dynamic font sizes, color accessibility, touch target optimization, and App Store accessibility requirements.
en.content:
Accessibility is not a feature—it's a responsibility. When you build with Rork Max, you're creating experiences that should be available to everyone, regardless of physical ability. This guide covers everything needed to make your Rork-generated apps fully accessible: screen readers, dynamic text, color blindness support, and App Store compliance.

Why Accessibility Matters

App Store Requirements: Apple now mandates accessibility compliance for app approval. Apps lacking proper accessibility support are rejected during review.

Legal Compliance: The US ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act), EU WCAG 2.1, and similar laws in other countries require digital accessibility. Non-compliance carries legal risk.

User Reach: About 15% of the global population (1.1+ billion people) have disabilities. Full accessibility opens your app to this significant market.

Business Impact: Better SEO, higher user satisfaction, stronger brand reputation, and reduced support burden from accessibility-related complaints.

accessibilityLabel: The Foundation

Every interactive element must have a semantic label. Screen reader users cannot see visual cues, so text labels are essential.

Best Practices for Labels:

  • Keep them concise (2–5 words ideal)
  • Don't include role names ("button", "link") — that's what accessibilityRole does
  • Use action verbs ("Add", "Delete", "Share")
  • Localize labels using translation files, not hardcoded strings

accessibilityHint: Provide Context

Where accessibilityLabel describes what, accessibilityHint explains why or what happens next. It's read after the user focuses the element.

Use hints sparingly—only when action consequences are unclear from the label alone.

accessibilityRole: Semantic Elements

Assign correct roles so screen readers interpret elements appropriately:

Available roles: button, link, header, image, imagebutton, keyboardkey, text, adjustable, checkbox, radio, switch, togglebutton, summary, search, spinbutton, tablist, tab, tabpanel, progressbar, menu, menuitem, none.

Screen Reader Navigation Order

Control the focus order so VoiceOver and TalkBack navigate logically through your app:

accessibilityViewIsModal: When true, focus cannot leave this view. Essential for modals, dialogs, and overlays.

Dynamic Type: Responsive Font Sizing

Users with vision impairment rely on large text. React Native supports dynamic text scaling via the fontScale property:

React Native's Text component defaults to allowFontScaling={true}, meaning text automatically scales with system settings. Never set allowFontScaling={false} unless absolutely necessary.

Test with maximum font scaling (Settings > Accessibility > Larger Sizes) to ensure layouts don't break.

Color Contrast: WCAG Standards

Text and background colors must have sufficient contrast. About 8% of people have color vision deficiency; they rely on contrast to distinguish content.

WCAG Contrast Ratios:

  • AA (Minimum): 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+)
  • AAA (Enhanced): 7:1 for normal text, 4.5:1 for large text

Check contrast with online tools like WebAIM Color Contrast Checker.

Test contrast in both light and dark modes.

Color Blindness: Don't Rely on Color Alone

About 1 in 12 men and 1 in 200 women have color blindness. Never communicate information using color alone.

Use the Color Blindness Simulator to preview how your app looks to people with color vision deficiency.

Touch Target Size: 44×44 Points

Mobile accessibility requires adequate touch targets. Apple and Google recommend a minimum of 44×44 points for all interactive elements:

hitSlop expands the touch-sensitive area without changing the visual size. Perfect for icon buttons where visual size must stay small.

ℹ️
**Spacing**: In addition to individual touch targets, maintain at least 8 points of spacing between interactive elements to prevent accidental taps.

Testing with VoiceOver (iOS)

Enable VoiceOver to test screen reader compatibility:

  1. Xcode Simulator:

    • Device → More Developer Tools → Accessibility Inspector
    • Or: Settings → Accessibility → VoiceOver → ON

  2. Physical Device:

    • Settings → Accessibility → VoiceOver → ON

VoiceOver Gestures:

  • Single tap: Select and read element
  • Double tap: Activate element (button press)
  • Two-finger swipe right: Next page
  • Two-finger swipe left: Previous page
  • Rotor (two-finger rotate): Jump between headings, links, etc.

Navigate your entire app with VoiceOver enabled. Verify:

  • All interactive elements are selectable
  • Labels are clear and descriptive
  • Navigation order is logical
  • Buttons and links activate correctly

Testing with TalkBack (Android)

Enable TalkBack for Android screen reader testing:

  1. Emulator or Device:

    • Settings → Accessibility → TalkBack → ON

  2. TalkBack Gestures:

    • Single tap: Select element
    • Double tap: Activate element
    • Swipe right: Next element
    • Swipe left: Previous element
    • Two-finger swipe right: Next page

Test the same scenarios as VoiceOver to ensure consistency across platforms.

Automated Accessibility Testing

Use Jest and React Native Testing Library to catch accessibility issues early:

Automated tests catch missing labels and incorrect roles, but manual VoiceOver/TalkBack testing is essential for real-world usability.

⚠️
**Manual testing is mandatory**: Automated tests cannot replicate the actual experience of navigating with a screen reader. Always test with VoiceOver and TalkBack enabled before submitting to the App Store.

App Store Review Checklist

Apple's accessibility review focuses on these areas:

  1. Screen Reader Support

    • [ ] All interactive elements have accessibilityLabel
    • [ ] VoiceOver navigation order is logical
    • [ ] Decorative images have accessible={false}
    • [ ] No elements are permanently hidden from screen readers

  2. Dynamic Type

    • [ ] Text scales at maximum font size
    • [ ] Layouts don't break at large text sizes
    • [ ] Minimum line height is at least 1.2× font size

  3. Color Contrast

    • [ ] All text meets WCAG AA (4.5:1) minimum
    • [ ] Dark mode contrast is also adequate
    • [ ] Charts and diagrams use patterns in addition to color

  4. Touch Targets

    • [ ] All buttons/links are 44×44pt or larger
    • [ ] Spacing between targets prevents accidental taps

  5. Keyboard Navigation

    • [ ] All functionality is accessible via keyboard
    • [ ] Tab order is logical
    • [ ] Focus indicators are visible

  6. App Store Connect Metadata

    • Complete the "Accessibility" section: 
      VoiceOver: Yes
Dynamic Type: Yes
Larger Sizes: Yes
Color Blindness Friendly: Yes

Common Rejection Reasons

  1. Screen Reader Incompatibility: Interactive elements don't respond to VoiceOver
  2. Insufficient Contrast: Text fails to meet WCAG AA ratio
  3. Small Touch Targets: Buttons are smaller than 44×44pt
  4. Missing Labels: Elements lack accessibilityLabel
  5. Focus Trap: VoiceOver gets stuck on specific elements
  6. Incomplete Metadata: Accessibility section in App Store Connect is empty

Implementation Checklist

  • [ ] All buttons have accessibilityLabel and accessibilityRole="button"
  • [ ] Text inputs have accessibilityRole="search" or "textbox"
  • [ ] Sections have headers with accessibilityRole="header"
  • [ ] Decorative images have accessible={false}
  • [ ] All text uses allowFontScaling={true} (default)
  • [ ] Line height is at least 1.2× font size for large text
  • [ ] Color contrast ratio is 4.5:1 or higher
  • [ ] Touch targets are 44×44pt minimum (including hitSlop)
  • [ ] Modals use accessibilityViewIsModal={true}
  • [ ] VoiceOver navigation order is logical
  • [ ] Dark mode has adequate contrast
  • [ ] App Store metadata includes accessibility features

Summary

Accessibility is not optional—it's essential. By implementing these practices, you ensure that everyone, regardless of ability, can use apps built with Rork Max. Beyond moral and legal obligations, accessible apps reach wider audiences and provide better UX for everyone.

Start with the fundamentals (labels, roles, contrast), test thoroughly with real screen readers, and iterate based on user feedback. Your commitment to accessibility will be reflected in app quality, user satisfaction, and App Store approval rates.

en.title: Complete Rork App Monetization Guide — Subscriptions, One-Time Purchases & Ads
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Monetize your Rork app with RevenueCat subscriptions, lifetime purchases, and AdMob ads. Covers paywall UI design, entitlement management, restore purchases, and App Store / Google Play review compliance.
en.content:
Introduction: Three Ways to Monetize a Rork App

Apps built with Rork can be monetized through three primary strategies:

  1. Subscriptions — Monthly/annual recurring billing (highest LTV)
  2. One-Time Purchase (Lifetime) — Pay once, access forever
  3. Ads (AdMob) — Revenue from free users

This guide focuses on RevenueCat-powered subscriptions + lifetime purchases — the combination that consistently generates the most sustainable revenue for indie app developers.

ℹ️
Rork is built on React Native, so RevenueCat's React Native SDK works out of the box. You'll need an Apple Developer Program account and/or a Google Play Console account to distribute paid apps.

1. RevenueCat Setup

Installation

Tell Rork to install RevenueCat via a prompt:

Add RevenueCat in-app purchases to this app.
Install react-native-purchases.
Configure with iOS and Android API keys.

Or add directly:

App Initialization (App.tsx)

2. Purchase State Management

3. Paywall UI

4. App Store Review Compliance

Common rejection reasons and how to avoid them:

5. Gating Premium Content

Summary

RevenueCat abstracts the complexity of Apple and Google billing APIs into a clean, unified SDK. The key principles for a successful monetization implementation:

  1. Entitlements control feature access — never check productId directly
  2. Restore Purchases is not optional — Apple will reject without it
  3. Pricing strategy matters more than implementation — annual plans with ~40% discount vs monthly consistently convert better than monthly-only

Track MRR, churn rate, and LTV in the RevenueCat dashboard to iterate on your pricing and paywall copy continuously.

en.title: Rork Max × EAS CI/CD Guide — EAS Build, EAS Update & OTA Deployments
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Set up a full CI/CD pipeline for Rork Max apps using Expo Application Services. Covers EAS Build (iOS/Android), EAS Update (OTA), GitHub Actions integration, and environment variable management with production examples.
en.content:
Introduction: What is Expo Application Services (EAS)?

Expo Application Services (EAS) is a managed cloud platform that automates the build and deployment of Rork Max applications—React Native / Expo-based mobile apps—without requiring local macOS, Xcode, or Android Studio environments. Traditionally, iOS builds demanded macOS with Xcode, while Android builds required Android Studio. With EAS, developers can build both iOS and Android apps from any machine: Linux servers, Windows PCs, or GitHub Actions runners.

Why EAS Matters for Rork Max Development

  1. Eliminates Local Environment Complexity: No need to install and maintain Xcode, Android Studio, or Xcode command-line tools
  2. Accelerates Release Cycles: Automated CI/CD pipelines reduce time from commit to production deployment
  3. Over-The-Air (OTA) Updates: Push JavaScript bundle fixes without waiting for App Store or Google Play review
  4. Streamlines Team Testing: Preview builds distributed via QR codes enable parallel QA testing
  5. Production Reliability: Automatic rollback capabilities, environment isolation, and multi-profile support ensure stable releases

This guide provides a complete, production-ready implementation of Rork Max with EAS CI/CD, including real-world code examples and best practices.

Chapter 1: EAS Setup and Initial Configuration

Step 1: Install EAS CLI Globally

Verify installation:

Step 2: Authenticate with Expo Account

You'll be prompted to open a browser and log in to your Expo account. If you don't have one, sign up for free at expo.dev.

ℹ️
**Pro Tip**: For CI/CD environments (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI), generate an API token at [expo.dev/settings/access-tokens](https://expo.dev/settings/access-tokens) and store it as the `EXPO_TOKEN` environment variable. This enables passwordless authentication across all pipelines.

Step 3: Initialize EAS Build Configuration

This command creates an eas.json configuration file in your project root. Below is a comprehensive example with development, preview, and production profiles.

Complete eas.json Configuration

Profile Breakdown

  • development: For local testing. Internal distribution via Expo Go
  • preview: For QA teams. Builds can be shared via QR code or internal testing tracks
  • production: For App Store / Google Play releases. Generates AAB (Android App Bundle) and App Store-ready iOS builds

Chapter 2: EAS Build for iOS

iOS Prerequisites

You'll need an Apple Developer Account to build for iOS. Sign up at developer.apple.com and enroll in the Apple Developer Program ($99/year).

Step 1: Configure iOS Credentials

This interactive command securely stores:

  • Apple ID email
  • App-specific password (recommended over account password)
  • Development and distribution certificates
  • Provisioning profiles
ℹ️
**Security First**: Never commit credentials to version control. EAS encrypts and manages all credentials server-side. For CI/CD, use `EXPO_TOKEN` instead of your Apple credentials.

Step 2: Create Your First iOS Build

EAS queues the build and displays a build ID. Monitor progress:

Upon completion, you'll receive a .ipa file ready for TestFlight submission.

Step 3: Submit to TestFlight

The app automatically uploads to App Store Connect and becomes available to TestFlight internal testers within minutes.

iOS Configuration Best Practices

Troubleshooting iOS Builds

If builds fail, check:

Common issues:

  • Certificate expired: Re-run eas credentials to regenerate
  • Provisioning profile mismatch: Ensure bundle ID matches provisioning profile
  • Xcode version conflict: EAS automatically uses the latest stable Xcode version

Chapter 3: EAS Build for Android

Android Prerequisites

You'll need a Google Play Developer Account to publish apps. Sign up at play.google.com/console ($25 one-time fee).

Step 1: Configure Android Keystore

EAS can auto-generate a keystore or import an existing one.

Manual Keystore Creation (if needed)

Register the keystore with EAS when prompted.

Step 2: Build APK or AAB

Chapter 4: EAS Update — OTA (Over-The-Air) Deployments

What OTA Updates Can Change

EAS Update distributes changes to JavaScript code and static assets without requiring new app store submissions.

Supported Updates:

  • React Native / Rork Max business logic

  • API calling logic
  • StyleSheet and CSS-in-JS styles

Not Supported:

**Production Practice**: Always validate OTA updates on a staging branch before production rollout. If issues are detected, rolling back takes seconds using `--rollback-to-release-id`.

Chapter 7: Preview Builds & QA Testing

Chapter 8: EAS Pricing & Plan Comparison

EAS Free Plan

  • Monthly Build Time: 30 minutes
  • Storage: 1 GB
  • Concurrent Builds: 1
  • Update Deployments: Unlimited
  • Support: Community

Ideal for: Prototyping, personal projects

EAS Pro Plan ($99/month)

  • Monthly Build Time: 500 minutes
  • Storage: 100 GB
  • Concurrent Builds: 2
  • Priority Support: Yes
  • Advanced Analytics: Yes

Ideal for: Actively developed apps with weekly+ releases

Cost Optimization Tips

Monthly Build Cost Estimate:

  • 1 weekly iOS + Android release: ~20 min/month (Free tier)
  • 2 weekly iOS + Android + OTA: ~60 min/month (Free tier exceeded, Pro recommended)
ℹ️
**Pro Tip**: Batch preview builds on a separate branch to separate QA testing from production release builds. This optimizes build queue usage and costs.

Chapter 9: Production Release Checklist

Before deploying Rork Max to production, verify:

Pre-Build Verification

  • [ ] eas.json production profile configured with correct settings
  • [ ] Apple Team ID and Google Play service account JSON registered
  • [ ] All required environment variables and secrets created in EAS
  • [ ] iOS certificates and provisioning profiles valid (not expired)
  • [ ] Android keystore properly configured and backed up
  • [ ] app.json version matches intended release version

Testing Phase

  • [ ] Preview build tested on iOS and Android devices
  • [ ] End-to-end tests pass (login, API calls, payments)
  • [ ] Staging API integration verified
  • [ ] Network error handling (offline, slow connection) tested
  • [ ] Performance profiling shows acceptable metrics
  • [ ] Crash analytics (Sentry, Crashlytics) configured

Release Execution

  • [ ] Git main branch updated with final code
  • [ ] npm run lint && npm run test passes locally and in CI
  • [ ] Version numbers updated in package.json and app.json
  • [ ] Release notes drafted and reviewed
  • [ ] Team members notified of pending deployment

Post-Deployment Monitoring

  • [ ] Crash rate monitored in Crashlytics/Sentry (< 0.1% acceptable)
  • [ ] Analytics dashboard shows expected session count
  • [ ] User reviews on App Store / Google Play reviewed
  • [ ] Support queue monitored for new issue reports
  • [ ] Rollback strategy prepared if critical bugs detected

Rollback Procedure

If critical issues emerge:

Summary: Key Takeaways

Implementing EAS CI/CD for Rork Max delivers:

  1. Simplified Development: No local Xcode/Android Studio required
  2. Faster Release Cycles: Automated build, test, and deploy pipelines
  3. Instant Hotfixes: OTA updates bypass app store review processes
  4. Parallel QA Testing: Preview builds enable team testing without new submissions
  5. Production Stability: Automated rollback, environment isolation, and comprehensive monitoring

By adopting EAS, your Rork Max team shifts focus from infrastructure management to feature development. Combined with GitHub Actions and environment-based configuration, you achieve a robust, scalable CI/CD platform that supports rapid iteration while maintaining production reliability.

en.title: Rork Max App Testing Guide — Jest, Detox E2E, and Quality Assurance
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Add production-grade testing to Rork Max generated apps. Covers Jest/React Native Testing Library for unit tests, Detox for E2E tests, snapshot testing, and CI automation on GitHub Actions.
en.content:
Introduction: Why Testing Matters for Rork Max Apps

Rork Max accelerates mobile app development by generating React Native code from AI prompts and visual builders. However, AI-generated code introduces unique testing challenges: auto-generated components may have subtle bugs, dependencies shift versions rapidly, and integration points require careful validation. Production-grade apps demand comprehensive testing, even when generated.

The testing pyramid for mobile apps follows three layers:

  1. Unit Tests (70%) — Test individual components, hooks, and utilities in isolation
  2. Integration Tests (20%) — Test multiple components working together, API calls
  3. E2E Tests (10%) — Test complete user flows on real/simulated devices

This guide covers all three using Jest (unit/integration) and Detox (E2E), integrated into GitHub Actions for continuous quality assurance.

ℹ️
Rork Max projects come pre-configured with Expo and Jest. This guide assumes you're starting from a generated Rork Max app structure (Expo managed workflow).

Part 1: Jest Setup for Unit Testing

Install Testing Dependencies

Your Rork Max project likely has Jest already, but ensure you have all necessary libraries:

Or with Yarn:

Configure jest.config.js

Create or update jest.config.js in your project root:

Create jest.setup.js

Run Your First Test

Create a simple test file app/__tests__/App.test.tsx:

Run tests:

⚠️
If you see "Cannot find module 'react-native'" errors, ensure `jest-expo` is the preset in jest.config.js. The `jest-expo` preset handles React Native mocking automatically.

Part 2: Component Unit Testing

Testing a Button Component

Most Rork Max apps include button components. Let's test a custom button:

Now test it:

Testing Forms

Forms are critical in apps. Test form submission:

Part 3: Hook Testing with renderHook
