Custom hooks are common in React Native apps. Test them with renderHook:

Test the hook:

Part 4: API and Service Mocking

Mocking external services (like Supabase) is essential. Use jest.mock():

Mock it in tests:

ℹ️
Always mock external services to avoid network calls during tests. Use `jest.clearAllMocks()` in `beforeEach` to reset mock state between tests.

Part 5: Snapshot Testing

Snapshot tests capture component output and detect regressions:

When you run npm test -- -u, Jest saves a snapshot file (ProfileCard.snapshot.test.tsx.snap). On subsequent runs, Jest compares output to the snapshot.

When to use snapshots:

  • ✅ UI components with stable structure
  • ✅ Regression detection for design changes

When to avoid:

  • ❌ Dynamic content (timestamps, IDs)
  • ❌ Frequently changing UI

Part 6: Detox E2E Setup

Detox tests apps end-to-end on real devices or simulators. Install:

Configure .detoxrc.js

Create .detoxrc.js in your project root:

Build the App

Run Your First E2E Test

Create e2e/firstTest.e2e.js:

Run the test:

⚠️
Detox requires Xcode and iOS simulators on macOS. For Android testing, use `android.emu.debug` configuration. Detox setup can be complex; refer to the official Detox docs for platform-specific issues.

Part 7: Detox E2E Test Examples

Login Flow Test

Navigation Flow Test

Form Submission Test

Part 8: Coverage Reporting

Generate Coverage Reports

Run Jest with coverage:

This generates coverage reports in coverage/ directory. Coverage metrics include:

  • Statements: Percentage of statements executed
  • Branches: Percentage of conditional branches tested
  • Functions: Percentage of functions called
  • Lines: Percentage of lines executed

Set Coverage Thresholds

In jest.config.js, set minimum thresholds:

If coverage falls below thresholds, Jest exits with code 1, failing the build.

View Coverage Report in Browser

After running coverage, open coverage/lcov-report/index.html in a browser to see detailed, interactive coverage reports.

Part 9: GitHub Actions CI Integration

Create .github/workflows/test.yml

Run on Every PR

The workflow automatically runs on pull requests. If tests fail, the PR shows a red X. If tests pass, it shows a green checkmark.

ℹ️
Add a badge to your README to show test status:
⚠️
macOS runners cost more than Linux. Consider running E2E tests only on main branch, not every PR, to reduce CI costs.

Summary: A Winning Testing Strategy for Rork Max

To ensure your Rork Max app is production-ready:

  1. Unit Tests (Jest): Cover components, hooks, and utilities. Aim for 80%+ line coverage.
  2. Component Tests: Test user interactions (button clicks, form submissions).
  3. Mocking: Mock external APIs and services to isolate logic.
  4. E2E Tests (Detox): Test critical user flows (login, payments, core features).
  5. CI/CD: Automate testing on every PR with GitHub Actions.
  6. Coverage Reports: Track and enforce coverage thresholds.

AI-generated code isn't perfect, but comprehensive testing catches issues early. Start with Jest unit tests, add E2E tests for critical paths, and iterate.

Next steps:

  • Set up Jest in your Rork Max project
  • Write tests for your most critical components
  • Integrate GitHub Actions for continuous testing
  • Gradually increase coverage as your app grows

Happy testing!

en.title: Rork Max Crash Reporting & Monitoring — Sentry and Firebase Crashlytics Integration
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Add crash reporting and error monitoring to Rork Max apps. Covers Sentry for React Native and Firebase Crashlytics setup, custom event tracking, user context, performance monitoring, and alert configuration.
en.content:
Rork Max Crash Reporting & Monitoring

In production, your Rork Max app will encounter errors you'll never see during development. A user on a slow network experiences a timeout. Another encounters an edge case you didn't anticipate. Without crash reporting, these errors vanish silently—users delete your app, and you never know why.

This guide walks you through integrating Sentry and Firebase Crashlytics with your Rork Max app, capturing real-world errors, tracking user impact, and setting up alerts to catch problems before your users do.

Why Crash Monitoring Matters

Every crash is an opportunity cost: app deletion, negative reviews, lost engagement. Without monitoring, you're flying blind.

What you can't see without monitoring:

  • Crash frequency: How many users are affected? Is it 0.1% or 10%?
  • User impact: Which users? Which devices or OS versions?
  • Error trends: Are crashes increasing after your latest release?
  • Root causes: Stack traces, network state, device memory at the moment of crash
  • Breadcrumbs: What actions led to the crash?
  • Performance degradation: Slow screens that don't quite crash but frustrate users

Crash reporting tools automatically capture, aggregate, and alert you to these issues, turning silent failures into actionable data.

Sentry vs Firebase Crashlytics

| Feature | Sentry | Firebase Crashlytics | |---------|--------|----------------------| | Crash Reporting | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Excellent | | Performance Monitoring | ✅ Built-in (Transactions) | ⚠️ Separate product (Performance) | | Error Breadcrumbs | ✅ Comprehensive | ⚠️ Basic | | User Context | ✅ Rich metadata support | ✅ Good | | Source Maps | ✅ Automatic upload | ✅ Automatic upload | | Free Tier | ✅ 5K events/month | ✅ Unlimited events | | Pricing | Pay-per-event ($29/month min) | Free + analytics features paid | | Setup Complexity | Low | Low | | React Native Support | ✅ First-class | ✅ First-class |

Choose Sentry if: You want advanced error grouping, performance monitoring, and detailed breadcrumbs—especially valuable for teams prioritizing observability.

Choose Firebase Crashlytics if: You're already using Firebase, prefer Google's ecosystem, and want a free solution with room to grow.

Sentry Integration with Rork Max

Step 1: Installation

Add Sentry to your Rork Max project using Expo:

ℹ️
Sentry's React Native SDK works seamlessly with Expo. If you're using EAS Build, Sentry will automatically handle source map uploads after your build completes.

Step 2: Initialize Sentry at App Entry

Create or update your app's entry point to initialize Sentry before any code runs. In Rork Max projects, this typically goes in app.json or your root component file:

Step 3: Capture Exceptions Manually

While Sentry automatically captures uncaught exceptions, you'll often want to capture errors explicitly:

Step 4: Capture Messages

For non-exception errors or important events:

Step 5: React Error Boundary

Wrap components that might crash to catch errors gracefully:

Sentry: User Context & Privacy

Tracking which user experienced an error is crucial for prioritization:

⚠️
**Privacy & GDPR**: Never send sensitive PII (passwords, credit cards, SSNs) to Sentry. Use anonymized user IDs where possible. Check your privacy policy and ensure users consent to error reporting.

Add additional context for debugging:

Sentry: Performance Monitoring

Track slow screens and operations:

The tracesSampleRate in your config controls what percentage of transactions are recorded. Set it to 1.0 in development to see all data, 0.1 in production to reduce noise.

Firebase Crashlytics Integration

Step 1: Install Dependencies

Configure Firebase in your app.json (or use google-services.json for Android):

Step 2: Initialize Crashlytics

Step 3: Record Errors

For non-fatal errors without exceptions:

Step 4: Custom Attributes & Breadcrumbs

Add context to crash reports:

Non-Fatal vs Fatal Errors: Strategy

Decide how to categorize errors:

| Error Type | Sentry | Crashlytics | Example | |-----------|--------|-------------|---------| | Fatal Crash | captureException() | Auto-captured | App freezes, force close | | Non-Fatal Exception | captureException() | recordError() | Network timeout caught and handled | | User Action Warning | captureMessage() | log() | Invalid form submission | | Performance Alert | Transactions (if slow) | n/a | Screen takes 5+ seconds to load |

Alerting & Notification Setup

Sentry Alerts

  1. Log in to sentry.io and go to your project

  2. Alerts → Create Alert Rule

  3. Choose conditions:

    • Error rate > 5% in last hour
    • New issue appears
    • Error count > 100

  4. Set actions:

    • Send to Slack: /slack https://hooks.slack.com/...
    • Email to team
    • PagerDuty integration for critical issues
Condition: "A new issue is created"
Actions: Slack channel #app-errors, Email team@company.com

Firebase Crashlytics Alerts

  1. Firebase Console → Crashlytics
  2. Setup → Alerts
  3. New Alert:
    • Crash-free user rate drops below 99%
    • Spike in crashes for a specific version
ℹ️
Both tools let you mute noisy errors (spam, intentional test crashes). Mark them as "resolved" to group future similar errors separately.

Choosing: Sentry vs Crashlytics for Your Team

Use Sentry if:

  • You want advanced error grouping and need to see patterns (e.g., "all crashes on older Android versions")
  • Your team requires performance monitoring (slow screens, HTTP request timing)
  • You want detailed breadcrumbs (last 10 user actions before crash)
  • You don't use Firebase and prefer a standalone solution
  • You need custom issue workflows and integrations

Use Firebase Crashlytics if:

  • You're already in the Google/Firebase ecosystem
  • You want a free solution with no per-event costs
  • Your crashes are primarily from native code (NDK errors)
  • You need simple, straightforward crash reporting without complexity
  • You already use Firebase Analytics and want unified reporting

Use Both if:

  • Your app is mission-critical and you can justify the complexity
  • You want diversity (if one service goes down, you still have data)
  • Sentry catches app crashes; Crashlytics logs native framework issues

Monitoring Checklist

Before shipping to production:

  • [ ] Sentry DSN configured for production environment
  • [ ] Source maps uploaded to both services (auto-upload on EAS Build enabled)
  • [ ] Release version set in Sentry config matching app.json
  • [ ] User context implemented—crashes show which user was affected
  • [ ] Error boundaries wrap risky components (deep navigation, image loading)
  • [ ] Try/catch blocks around async operations (network calls, file operations)
  • [ ] Alert rules created for error spikes (> 5% increase)
  • [ ] Slack/email notifications tested
  • [ ] Privacy policy updated to disclose error reporting
  • [ ] Sample errors sent from staging to verify both services receive data
  • [ ] Team members have dashboard access and know how to respond

Summary

Crash reporting transforms invisible errors into actionable data. Sentry excels at observability and performance; Crashlytics offers a free, Google-integrated alternative.

For Rork Max apps:

  1. Start with one: Pick Sentry for advanced features or Crashlytics for simplicity
  2. Instrument gradually: Begin with automatic crash capture, add user context, then performance monitoring
  3. Set alerts early: Catch spikes before they impact users at scale
  4. Review regularly: Weekly check-ins on the crash dashboard catch trends early
  5. Privacy first: Get user consent and avoid logging sensitive data

Your users won't notice crash reporting—until you fix a bug they reported three weeks ago. Then they'll appreciate the responsiveness.

Next steps:

  • Create a Sentry or Firebase account (both offer free trials)
  • Add the SDK to your Rork Max project
  • Send test crashes from staging and verify they appear in dashboards
  • Configure alerts and invite your team

en.title: Implementing Dynamic Island and Live Activities with Rork Max
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build Dynamic Island and Live Activities into your iOS app using Rork Max. Step-by-step tutorial with Swift code examples, real-world use cases, and FAQ.
en.content:
What Are Dynamic Island and Live Activities — and Why Rork Max Makes Them Accessible

The Dynamic Island, introduced with iPhone 14 Pro, transforms the front-camera cutout into a living, interactive UI surface. Combined with Live Activities, it lets your app display real-time, persistent information — delivery status, sports scores, flight countdowns — directly on the lock screen and in the pill-shaped island at the top of the display.

Until Rork Max, building these features required deep expertise in Swift, SwiftUI, and Apple's ActivityKit framework. Rork Max generates native Swift/SwiftUI code from plain English prompts, giving any developer access to even the most advanced iOS APIs.

In this guide you'll learn how to implement Dynamic Island and Live Activities in a food delivery app using Rork Max — from setting up the Widget Extension target to pushing real-time updates from your server.

Prerequisites and Setup

Before diving in, confirm you have everything in place.

What you need:

  • Rork Max subscription — Dynamic Island requires native Swift APIs only available in Rork Max (not the React Native–based Rork free plan)
  • An iPhone 14 Pro or later for on-device testing, or the Xcode Simulator running iOS 16.1+
  • An Apple Developer account to obtain push tokens for remote Live Activity updates

iOS version requirements:

  • Live Activities (lock screen only): iOS 16.1+
  • Live Activities + Dynamic Island: iOS 16.2+
  • Full Dynamic Island animations: iOS 16.2+

Rork Max generates native Swift code compiled on cloud-hosted Macs, giving your app full access to ActivityKit, WidgetKit, and the entire Apple platform. For a broader overview of what Rork Max can target, see Rork Max Apple Multi-Platform Guide.

How Live Activities Work Under the Hood

Live Activities are powered by ActivityKit — a framework that manages the lifecycle of ongoing real-time widgets. Here's the conceptual model:

Rork Max will generate code like this automatically — you just describe what you want in plain English.

Writing Effective Rork Max Prompts for Dynamic Island

The key to getting great Dynamic Island UI from Rork Max is being specific about each display region. Dynamic Island has four distinct regions you can populate:

| Region | Description | |---|---| | compactLeading | Left side of the compact pill | | compactTrailing | Right side of the compact pill | | minimal | Tiny icon shown when competing with another app's activity | | Expanded | Full interactive view when the user long-presses the island |

Example prompt for a delivery app:

"Add a Live Activity to this food delivery app. In the compact Dynamic Island view, show the remaining time (number + 'min') on the left and a bicycle icon on the right. In the expanded view, show an order progress bar with four steps (Preparing → Picked Up → On the Way → Delivered) and the estimated arrival time. Mirror the same information on the lock screen in a larger layout."

Rork Max generates the full SwiftUI widget from this prompt:

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Add the Widget Extension Target

Prompt Rork Max to set up the required Xcode target and entitlements:

"Add a Widget Extension target named 'DeliveryWidget' to this project. Enable Live Activities by setting NSSupportsLiveActivities to true in the app's Info.plist."

Rork Max will configure:

  • A new Widget Extension target with WidgetKit + ActivityKit
  • NSSupportsLiveActivities: YES in Info.plist
  • Shared App Group for data passing between the app and widget

Step 2: Build the Activity Manager

Step 3: Enable Server-Side Push Updates

For production apps, your server pushes Live Activity updates directly via APNs without requiring the app to be open. For the full push notification setup, see Push Notifications with Rork Max.

The Live Activity push payload format differs from standard notifications:

Key fields:

  • event: "update" to refresh state, "end" to dismiss the activity
  • content-state: Must match your ContentState Codable structure exactly
  • stale-date: Unix timestamp after which the activity is marked as stale if not updated

Step 4: Apple Watch Integration

Rork Max can also generate Apple Watch complications that mirror your Live Activity data. Ask Rork Max:

"Add a Watch complication that shows the same delivery status and ETA as the iPhone Live Activity, updating via WatchConnectivity."

For a detailed Watch integration tutorial, see Rork Max Apple Watch App Guide.

Common Errors and How to Fix Them

Problem: Live Activity doesn't appear

Check that NSSupportsLiveActivities is set to YES in your app's Info.plist (not the widget target's plist). Also verify the user hasn't disabled Live Activities in Settings → [Your App] → Live Activities.

Problem: Dynamic Island doesn't expand on tap

Dynamic Island expansion requires the device to run iOS 16.2+ on iPhone 14 Pro or later. On older devices, the activity appears as a banner and on the lock screen — the Dynamic Island UI is silently ignored. Always test on the correct simulator model.

Problem: Activity.request throws an error

iOS limits concurrent Live Activities to 5 per app. End stale activities before starting new ones:

Problem: Push updates aren't reflected in the Dynamic Island

Ensure the APNs topic uses the .liveactivity suffix: com.yourcompany.yourapp.push-type.liveactivity. Also confirm the push token is refreshed — Live Activity push tokens rotate and must be re-sent to your server when they change.

Real-World Use Cases

Dynamic Island and Live Activities are particularly compelling for these app categories:

Delivery & Logistics — Show package ETA, driver location, and step-by-step status updates. Users never need to open the app to check where their order is.

Fitness & Health — Display active workout metrics (heart rate, pace, calories) on the lock screen during a run. Pair with HealthKit data Rork Max can access natively.

Finance & Payments — Show a countdown timer for a pending bank transfer, or a live exchange rate for an active crypto trade.

Travel — Gate changes, boarding countdowns, and real-time flight delay notifications without requiring push notification permission for every update.

Sports — Live scores, down-and-distance in American football, or ball-by-ball cricket updates — all surfaced on the lock screen without needing the app open.

FAQ

Do I need Rork Max to implement Dynamic Island, or can I use the free Rork plan?

You need Rork Max. The free Rork plan generates React Native (Expo) code which cannot access native Swift APIs like ActivityKit or WidgetKit. Dynamic Island and Live Activities are exclusively available through Rork Max's Swift/SwiftUI code generation.

How many Live Activities can run at the same time?

iOS 16 allows up to 5 concurrent Live Activities per app. From iOS 17 onward, Apple increased this limit. However, it's best practice to end completed activities promptly to conserve battery and avoid confusing the user.

Can I update a Live Activity when the app is in the background or closed?

Yes — that's the primary advantage of Live Activities. Once you have the push token, your server can send updates directly to Apple's servers (APNs), which relay them to the device regardless of whether the app is running. The activity continues displaying until you explicitly end it or it reaches its maximum duration (8 hours by default, extendable to 12 hours if the user keeps the app in the foreground).

Does Rork Max support the new ActivityKit API introduced in iOS 18?

Yes. Rork Max stays current with the latest iOS SDK. If you want to target a specific iOS version, include it in your prompt — for example: "Use the iOS 18 ActivityKit API with the updated ContentMargins and tinted icons support."

How do I test Live Activities without a physical iPhone?

Use Xcode Simulator with an iPhone 14 Pro or later model selected (Simulator → Device). You can simulate Live Activity state changes directly from Xcode's Debug menu → Simulate Live Activity. Remote push testing requires a physical device and a valid APNs token.

Summary

Rork Max opens the door to one of iOS's most engaging native features — Dynamic Island and Live Activities — without writing a line of Swift by hand. Here's what you've learned:

  • Dynamic Island has four distinct regions (compact leading/trailing, minimal, expanded) that you can describe in plain English to Rork Max
  • ActivityAttributes separates static data (set once) from dynamic ContentState (updated throughout the activity)
  • The LiveActivityManager pattern handles start, update, and end lifecycle cleanly
  • Server-side push updates via APNs let your backend drive the Live Activity without requiring the app to be open
  • Apple Watch integration via WatchConnectivity extends the same experience to the wrist

Whether you're building a delivery app, a fitness tracker, or a live sports companion, Rork Max makes implementing Dynamic Island and Live Activities as straightforward as describing what you want. Give it a try and ship something your users will notice every time they glance at their phone.

en.title: Implementing QR Code Scanning and Generation with Rork Max: A Complete Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to implement QR code scanning and generation in your Rork Max iOS app. Covers AVFoundation-based camera scanning, CoreImage QR generation, scan history management, and real-world use cases.
en.content:
Introduction

QR codes are essential in modern mobile apps — from payment systems and loyalty cards to inventory management and event ticketing. With Rork Max, you can implement a high-accuracy QR code scanner powered by iOS's native AVFoundation framework and a QR code generator using CoreImage, all through natural language prompts.

This guide covers:

  • Building a QR code scanner with AVFoundation
  • Generating QR codes with CoreImage
  • Managing scan history with UserDefaults
  • Real-world use cases (event check-in, WiFi sharing, and more)

How QR Code Scanning Works on iOS

iOS QR code scanning uses the following pipeline:

  1. AVCaptureSession initializes the camera input stream
  2. AVCaptureMetadataOutput detects QR code patterns in the camera feed
  3. The detected QR code data is extracted and processed
  4. SwiftUI overlays provide visual scanning feedback to the user

Rork Max generates all of this native Swift code from a single prompt.

Step 1: Implement the QR Code Scanner

Prompt for Rork Max

Enter the following prompt in the Rork Max editor:

Add a QR code scanner to my iOS app.
Requirements:
- AVFoundation-based camera QR code reader
- Haptic and sound feedback on successful scan
- Display scan result in a bottom sheet modal
- Show "Open in Safari" button when the scanned value is a URL
- Save up to 20 scan results to history (UserDefaults)
- Torch on/off toggle button
- Include camera permission request handling

Example of Generated Code

Here is the core structure Rork Max generates for the QR scanner:

ℹ️
You must add `NSCameraUsageDescription` to `Info.plist`. Rork Max automatically includes this key when generating camera-related code.

Step 2: Implement QR Code Generation

Beyond scanning, many apps need to generate QR codes to display or share with other users.

Prompt for Rork Max

Add QR code generation to my app.
Requirements:
- Text field for entering any string or URL
- Generate and display a QR code using CoreImage filters
- Save the generated QR code to the photo library
- Share functionality using UIActivityViewController
- Generate QR codes at 256x256 pixels

Generated QR Code Logic

Displaying in SwiftUI

💡
Setting `.interpolation(.none)` is crucial for QR codes — it keeps pixel boundaries sharp and prevents the scanner from failing to read blurry codes.

Step 3: Managing Scan History

Saving and displaying scan history improves usability for apps that scan codes frequently.

Real-World Use Cases

Use Case 1: Event Check-In App

Use Case 2: WiFi Password Sharing via QR Code

Performance Best Practices

Keep these tips in mind when integrating QR code features:

Battery efficiency: Always call session.stopRunning() after a successful scan or when the view disappears. Rork Max automatically adds this to onDisappear lifecycle hooks.

Duplicate scan prevention: Add a brief cooldown (around 0.5 seconds) after a successful scan to avoid processing the same code multiple times.

Thread safety: AVFoundation metadata callbacks are delivered on a background thread. Always dispatch UI updates to the main thread using DispatchQueue.main.async.

QR code rendering sharpness: When displaying generated QR codes, always use .interpolation(.none) and white background — dark mode or gray backgrounds can cause scanners to fail to read the code.

FAQ

Q1. Can I implement QR code scanning with Rork (free tier)?

Yes, but with a different tech stack. The free Rork tier uses Expo React Native, so it leverages expo-barcode-scanner for QR scanning. The AVFoundation-based native Swift implementation described in this article is specific to Rork Max (native Swift code generation).

Q2. Can I scan barcodes other than QR codes (like EAN-13 or Code128)?

Yes. In your Rork Max prompt, specify "multi-format scanner supporting EAN-13, QR Code, and Code128." Rork Max will add those formats to the metadataObjectTypes array in AVCaptureMetadataOutput, enabling recognition of those barcode types.

Q3. Can I automatically open scanned URLs in Safari?

Yes. You can ask Rork Max to add logic that calls UIApplication.shared.open(url) when the scanned value is a URL. However, as a best practice, it's better to show a confirmation dialog first — auto-launching external URLs without user confirmation creates a poor user experience and may cause App Store review issues.

Summary

Rork Max makes it straightforward to ship production-quality QR code features using iOS's native AVFoundation and CoreImage frameworks — no manual Swift coding required. With the patterns covered in this guide, you can quickly build QR functionality for payment apps, event management systems, inventory tools, and more.

For further reading, check out these related articles:

  • Camera and Photo Library in Rork Max
  • Rork Max Native API Guide
  • Stripe Payment Integration with Rork Max

en.title: Rork × GitHub Integration Guide: From Code Export to Version Control
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to export your Rork projects to GitHub and manage them with version control. Covers CI/CD with GitHub Actions, branching strategies, and team workflows.
en.content:
Why Connect Rork to GitHub?

Rork lets you generate fully functional mobile apps from plain-English prompts — but as your project grows toward production, you need robust version control and team collaboration workflows. GitHub is the standard answer, and Rork makes it easy to connect.

Rork-generated code is built on React Native with Expo, meaning it's just standard open-source code you own entirely. Putting that code under GitHub version control unlocks:

  • Change history — see exactly what the AI generated vs. what you changed manually
  • Rollback safety — revert to any previous working version in seconds
  • Team reviews — designers and engineers can code-review AI-generated output
  • CI/CD automation — trigger builds and tests automatically on every push
  • Third-party integrations — connect Vercel, Cloudflare, EAS, and other tools seamlessly

This guide walks you through the complete workflow: exporting from Rork, setting up a GitHub repository, managing branches, and automating builds with GitHub Actions.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have the following:

  • A Rork account (code export is available on all plans)
  • A GitHub account (free)
  • Node.js 18 or later installed locally
  • Expo CLI (npm install -g @expo/cli)
  • The git command-line tool

Note: If you're using Rork Max (native Swift), the export flow differs — this guide focuses on the standard Rork (React Native/Expo) workflow.

Step 1: Export Your Code and Connect to GitHub

1-1. Export from the Rork Dashboard

Open your Rork project dashboard and click the "Export Code" or "GitHub" button in the top-right corner.

Rork offers two export methods:

Method A: ZIP Download Download the entire project as a ZIP file. Extract it locally and push to your own GitHub repository.

Method B: GitHub OAuth Integration (Recommended) Authorize Rork via GitHub OAuth, then select a target repository. From that point on, every time you run a prompt, Rork automatically commits the resulting diff to your repository.

1-2. Create a New GitHub Repository

1-3. Push Your Initial Commit

If you used the ZIP download method, follow these steps to make your first commit:

After pushing, verify the React Native/Expo project structure is visible in your GitHub repository.

Step 2: Branching Strategy for Rork Projects

Because Rork can regenerate large portions of code with each prompt, a solid branching strategy is essential to keep your main branch stable.

2-1. Recommended Branch Structure

main        ← Production releases (stable)
├── develop ← Integration branch for ongoing work
│   ├── rork/feature-login     ← AI-generated login screen
│   ├── rork/feature-profile   ← AI-generated profile page
│   └── fix/bugfix-crash       ← Manual bug fix

2-2. Commit Message Conventions

Use prefixes to clearly distinguish AI-generated code from manual changes:

| Prefix | Meaning | |--------|---------| | rork: | Code generated by a Rork prompt | | fix: | Bug fix | | feat: | Manually added feature | | style: | UI/design adjustments | | chore: | Config changes, dependency updates |

Step 3: Automate Builds with GitHub Actions + EAS

Combine GitHub Actions with Expo Application Services (EAS) to trigger automatic builds on every push to main.

3-1. Configure Expo EAS

This creates an eas.json configuration file. Review the build profiles:

3-2. Create a GitHub Actions Workflow

Setting up the GitHub Secret:

  1. Generate an API token in Expo Dashboard under Settings > Access Tokens
  2. Add it to your GitHub repo at Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions as EXPO_TOKEN

Now every push to main triggers an EAS build — you can monitor status in the Expo dashboard.

Common Errors and Fixes

Here are the most frequent issues when connecting Rork code to GitHub, and how to resolve them.

Error: failed to push some refs to 'origin'

Cause: Local and remote histories have diverged.

Error: npm ci fails during CI build

Cause: The package-lock.json exported by Rork may be outdated.

Error: EXPO_TOKEN authentication failure

Cause: Token has expired or has insufficient permissions. Generate a new token in the Expo dashboard and update the GitHub secret.

Large unexpected diffs after a Rork prompt

Rork sometimes regenerates the full codebase with each prompt. Use git diff --stat to review the scope before committing, and always work on a feature branch rather than directly on main.

Advanced: Team Collaboration Tips

When working with a team on a Rork project, the following practices make a big difference.

Pull Request Template

Create .github/pull_request_template.md to standardize reviews of AI-generated code:

Protect Main with Branch Rules

In your repository settings, enable Branch protection rules for main:

  • Require pull request reviews before merging
  • Require status checks (EAS build) to pass
  • Restrict direct pushes to main

This ensures every Rork-generated change is reviewed before it reaches production.

FAQ

Q: Can I use GitHub integration on the Rork free plan?

A: ZIP code download is available on all Rork plans including free. The GitHub OAuth integration (direct push from Rork on every prompt) requires a paid plan. Free-plan users can manually push ZIPs to GitHub following the steps in Step 1-3.

Q: If I manually edit the exported code, will a new Rork prompt overwrite my changes?

A: Yes — Rork regenerates the full project on each prompt, which can overwrite manual edits. Keep custom modifications in separate branches (prefix them with custom/) and merge carefully after each Rork-generated update.

Q: Can I use private GitHub repositories?

A: Absolutely. When granting Rork access via GitHub OAuth, you can select private repositories. Always use private repos for projects containing proprietary business logic or API integrations.

Q: How long do EAS builds take in GitHub Actions?

A: iOS preview builds (simulator) typically take 5–15 minutes; production builds take 15–30 minutes depending on EAS queue load. For teams needing faster parallel builds, consider an EAS premium plan.

Summary

Connecting Rork to GitHub bridges the gap between AI-powered rapid prototyping and professional software development practices. Here's a quick recap of what we covered:

GitHub integration gives Rork-generated React Native code full version control — every AI-generated diff becomes a reviewable, revertible commit. A clear branching strategy (rork/feature-xxx branches off develop) keeps AI output separate from manual changes and ensures nothing untested reaches main. GitHub Actions combined with Expo EAS automates your entire build pipeline, so every push to main triggers a cloud build without any manual effort.

For next steps, check out the App Store Publishing Guide to take your version-controlled app all the way to release. For performance tuning, see Advanced Rork App Performance Optimization. And for setting up your backend, the Supabase & Firebase Integration Guide has you covered.

en.title: How to Implement Live Activities & Dynamic Island with Rork Max
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to add Live Activities and Dynamic Island to your iOS app using Rork Max — no Swift expertise required. Build a food delivery tracker with step-by-step prompts.
en.content:
Introduction

Live Activities and Dynamic Island are two of the most impactful UI features Apple has shipped in recent years. Since iOS 16.1, apps can surface real-time data — delivery countdowns, sports scores, ride-sharing ETAs — right on the Lock Screen and in the Dynamic Island pill at the top of the display. Users never need to open your app to stay informed.

The traditional challenge? Implementing ActivityKit in Swift has been a multi-day project: you need to define ActivityAttributes, build separate SwiftUI views for each Dynamic Island presentation mode, wire up push token handling for server-side updates, and keep everything in sync with your app's state. That complexity has kept many indie developers from shipping the feature.

Rork Max changes the equation. Because it generates native Swift code from plain-language prompts, you can have a working Live Activities integration — including all three Dynamic Island modes — in a few focused prompt iterations. This guide walks you through the complete process using a food delivery app as a concrete, testable example.

What you'll learn:

  • How Live Activities and Dynamic Island work under the hood
  • How to structure your prompts for ActivityKit code generation
  • Step-by-step implementation of a delivery tracking Live Activity
  • Common errors and how to fix them with follow-up prompts

Prerequisites: Basic familiarity with Rork Max. New to Rork? Start with our Getting Started guide first.

How Live Activities and Dynamic Island Work

Live Activities

Live Activities use Apple's ActivityKit framework to display a persistent, real-time widget on the Lock Screen and Notification Center. They're designed for tasks with a clear start and end — a 30-minute food delivery, a 90-minute match, a Pomodoro session. Apple caps them at 8 hours maximum.

Each Live Activity is defined by an ActivityAttributes struct with two distinct parts:

  • Static attributes — data that never changes during the activity (restaurant name, order ID)
  • ContentState — mutable data pushed as updates (delivery status, ETA, driver name)

Dynamic Island

The Dynamic Island (iPhone 14 Pro and later) takes the same ActivityAttributes but renders three distinct UI modes:

| Mode | When shown | Approximate size | |------|-----------|-----------------| | Compact | Another app is in the foreground | ~120×36pt | | Minimal | Two activities running simultaneously | Very small pill | | Expanded | User long-presses the island | Up to 160pt tall |

All three views share the same live ContentState, so every update to the activity automatically propagates across every surface. You manage one data model — ActivityKit handles the rest.

Setting Up Your Project in Rork Max

Navigate to rork.com/max and create a new project. Your opening prompt should describe the full scope so Rork Max can scaffold the right file structure from the start:

Rork Max will generate a project structure including:

  • DeliveryActivityAttributes.swift — ActivityKit data model
  • DeliveryLiveActivityView.swift — Lock Screen / Notification Center UI
  • DeliveryDynamicIslandView.swift — Dynamic Island UI (all three modes)
  • OrderViewModel.swift — business logic for starting, updating, and ending the activity

Step-by-Step Implementation

Step 1: Review the Generated ActivityAttributes

Rork Max produces an ActivityAttributes conformance similar to this:

Expected output: The file compiles without errors. ContentState conforms to both Codable and Hashable — both are required by ActivityKit.

Step 2: Refine the Dynamic Island UI

The default generated UI is functional but minimal. Use a follow-up prompt to upgrade each mode:

Rork Max will produce a DynamicIsland view block like this:

Step 3: Implement the Full Activity Lifecycle

The generated OrderViewModel.swift handles starting, updating, and ending the activity. Here are the key methods:

Common Errors and How to Fix Them

"NSSupportsLiveActivities missing from Info.plist"

Prompt Rork Max: "Add NSSupportsLiveActivities set to true in Info.plist." This is a required entitlement that Rork Max sometimes omits on first generation.

Dynamic Island not visible in simulator

Select an iPhone 14 Pro (or later) simulator. Dynamic Island does not exist on earlier hardware models or their simulators.

Activity.request() throws immediately

Usually a Codable conformance issue in ContentState. If any property in the struct doesn't fully conform to Codable — including nested enums without raw values — ActivityKit throws at runtime. Prompt: "Fix any Codable conformance issues in DeliveryActivityAttributes.ContentState."

ETA timer shows 0:00 instead of counting down

Text(date, style: .timer) requires the date to be in the future at render time. If your ETA calculation produces a past date, the timer shows 0:00. Double-check that Date().addingTimeInterval(...) produces a future timestamp.

Advanced: Server-Driven Updates via APNs

Once your backend is set up, you can update Live Activities server-side via Apple Push Notification service (APNs) — no app wake-up required. Ask Rork Max:

The generated code retrieves the activity's pushToken and shows the expected JSON payload shape, which your server sends to the APNs Live Activities endpoint. This is the production pattern used by apps like Uber and DoorDash to push real-time location updates directly to the Lock Screen.

For a broader look at native Apple APIs available in Rork Max, see Using Native Apple APIs with Rork Max. To ship your app across the full Apple ecosystem — Apple Watch, Apple TV, Vision Pro — check out Building Multi-Platform Apple Apps with Rork Max.

FAQ

Do Live Activities work on Android?

No — Live Activities and Dynamic Island are iOS-only features. For Android users, Rork (the standard React Native version) supports foreground service notifications with custom layouts, which serve a similar purpose.

What's the maximum duration for a Live Activity?

Apple enforces an 8-hour maximum. After that, the system automatically dismisses the activity. Always call activity.end() when the underlying task completes — leaving a stale delivery tracker on someone's Lock Screen is poor UX.

Can I test Dynamic Island without a physical device?

Yes. Xcode simulators running iOS 16.1+ correctly preview the Lock Screen Live Activity UI. For Dynamic Island specifically, you need an iPhone 14 Pro (or later) simulator. Rork Max's browser-based streaming simulator also supports Lock Screen preview.

Summary

Live Activities and Dynamic Island are high-value engagement features that most indie apps skip because the implementation overhead outweighs the benefits for small teams. Rork Max removes that overhead — the hard part shifts from debugging Swift generics to writing clear prompts.

Key takeaways:

  1. Define ActivityAttributes and ContentState precisely in your first prompt
  2. Request all three Dynamic Island modes (compact, minimal, expanded) explicitly in a follow-up
  3. Always implement the full lifecycle: start, update, and end
  4. Add NSSupportsLiveActivities to Info.plist — prompt Rork Max if it's missing

Next up: complement your Live Activity with Home Screen and Lock Screen widgets, or explore Rork Max prompt engineering tips to squeeze more out of every iteration.

en.title: Rork vs Bolt: The Ultimate AI App Builder Comparison (2026)
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: A deep-dive comparison of Rork and Bolt across features, pricing, and ease of use. Find out which AI builder is right for your next app project in 2026.
en.content:
Introduction: Choosing the Right AI App Builder

The AI-powered app development landscape has exploded in 2026. You no longer need to write thousands of lines of code to ship a production-quality app — tools like Rork and Bolt let you describe what you want in plain English and watch your app come to life in minutes.

Both tools promise to simplify app creation, but they serve fundamentally different needs. Rork focuses on native mobile apps for iOS and Android, while Bolt is purpose-built for fast web application development. Choosing the wrong tool can cost you days of wasted effort.

In this guide, you'll learn:

  • What sets Rork and Bolt apart
  • How each handles mobile vs. web development
  • Pricing and plan breakdowns
  • Real-world workflows and code examples
  • Which tool is right for your project

What Is Rork?

Rork is an AI-native mobile app builder that generates fully functional iOS and Android apps using React Native. You describe your app in natural language, and Rork's AI writes the code, sets up navigation, connects your backend, and even pushes to the App Store — all without touching Xcode or Android Studio.

In February 2026, Rork launched Rork Max, a premium tier that generates native Swift apps instead of React Native. Rork Max targets the full Apple ecosystem: iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, Apple TV, and Vision Pro, unlocking capabilities like AR/LiDAR, HealthKit, Core ML, and Metal-powered 3D graphics.

Key Features

  • Mobile-first: Build apps destined for the App Store and Google Play
  • React Native and Swift: Cross-platform or fully native Apple apps
  • Fix Now: AI-powered automatic bug detection and repair
  • Full code ownership: Export your source code anytime, no lock-in
  • Backend integrations: Supabase, Firebase, Stripe, and more built-in

What Is Bolt?

Bolt is an AI-powered web app builder developed by StackBlitz. It uses WebContainer technology to spin up fully functional web applications directly in your browser — no local setup required. You type a prompt, and within seconds a working prototype appears.

Bolt's superpower is speed. It's the fastest way to go from idea to a running web prototype, and it supports a wide range of JavaScript/TypeScript frameworks including React, Next.js, Vue, and Svelte.

Key Features

  • Web-first: Build SPAs, full-stack apps, dashboards, and SaaS tools
  • Blazing fast: Prototypes are live within seconds
  • No installation needed: Everything runs in the browser
  • Multi-framework support: React, Next.js, Vue, Svelte, and more
  • Live code editing: Edit the generated code directly in an integrated IDE

Feature-by-Feature Comparison

Platform Support

| Feature | Rork | Bolt | |---------|------|------| | iOS app | ✅ React Native / Swift | ❌ | | Android app | ✅ React Native | ❌ | | Web app | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Full support | | App Store publishing | ✅ 2-click | ❌ | | Google Play publishing | ✅ Supported | ❌ | | Browser execution | ❌ | ✅ | | Apple Watch / Vision Pro | ✅ (Rork Max) | ❌ |

The platform difference is the single most important factor in choosing between these two tools.

AI Capabilities

| Feature | Rork | Bolt | |---------|------|------| | Natural language input | ✅ | ✅ | | Automatic bug repair | ✅ Fix Now | ⚠️ Manual at times | | Real-time preview | ✅ On physical device | ✅ In browser | | Component generation | ✅ React Native / Swift UI | ✅ Web UI | | API schema generation | ✅ | ⚠️ Partial | | Code export | ✅ | ✅ |

Backend & Infrastructure

| Service | Rork | Bolt | |---------|------|------| | Supabase | ✅ | ✅ | | Firebase | ✅ | ✅ | | Stripe | ✅ | ⚠️ Partial | | Cloudflare Workers | ⚠️ Partial | ✅ | | GitHub integration | ✅ | ✅ |

Pricing

Rork (as of March 2026)

  • Free tier: 35 credits/month (5 credits/day cap)
  • Pro plan: Starting at $25/month — 100+ credits, no daily limit
  • Rork Max: Higher-tier plan for native Apple development

Keep in mind that publishing to the App Store requires an Apple Developer account ($99/year) and Google Play requires a one-time $25 registration fee.

Bolt (as of March 2026)

  • Free tier: Limited monthly credits
  • Pro plan: Starting at ~$20/month
  • Team plan: Multi-seat collaboration

Real Development Workflows

Building a Mobile App with Rork

// Example: Task management app with authentication

1. Sign in at rork.com
2. Type: "Build a task management app with user login,
   task creation/editing, due dates, and push notifications"
3. Rork AI generates React Native code (~1–2 minutes)
4. Preview on your phone via the Rork iOS/Android app
5. Iterate: "Add a dark mode toggle" → AI updates in real time
6. Tap Fix Now if any errors appear → auto-repaired
7. Publish to App Store in 2 clicks

// Expected output:
// - Supabase auth (sign up / sign in / sign out)
// - Task CRUD with due date picker
// - Push notification scheduling
// - Cross-platform (iOS + Android) from a single codebase

The entire flow — from first prompt to a live App Store listing — can realistically be completed in a single day.

Building a Web App with Bolt

// Example: Task management web app

1. Visit bolt.new
2. Type: "Create a task manager web app with authentication,
   task CRUD, due dates, and a kanban board view"
3. Bolt generates a Next.js + Tailwind CSS app (in seconds)
4. Preview directly in browser — no phone needed
5. Iterate: "Make the sidebar collapsible"
6. Deploy to Vercel with one click
7. Share the URL with teammates instantly

// Expected output:
// - Next.js 15 app with Supabase auth
// - Kanban board with drag-and-drop
// - Responsive design out of the box
// - Deployed at a public URL within minutes

Common Issues and How to Fix Them

With Rork

  • Out of credits mid-build: Optimize your prompts to be more specific upfront, or upgrade your plan. Batch related changes into single prompts rather than one change per message.
  • Device preview not launching: Restart the Rork companion app, or switch to Expo Go for testing.
  • Supabase RLS errors: Check that your Row Level Security policies allow the authenticated user to read/write the relevant tables.

With Bolt

  • Generated code becomes too complex to edit: Break your prompts into smaller, incremental requests. Avoid asking for many unrelated features in one prompt.
  • Fix-break loop where bugs never resolve: Create a new project branch and regenerate the problematic component from scratch.
  • App works locally but breaks after deploy: Verify that all environment variables (API keys, database URLs) are configured in your deployment platform (Vercel, Netlify).

When to Choose Rork vs Bolt

Choose Rork if:

  • You want a native iOS or Android app that can be published to the App Store or Google Play
  • You need access to device-level APIs: camera, GPS, HealthKit, push notifications, Face ID, NFC
  • You're targeting the full Apple ecosystem — iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, Apple TV, Vision Pro (Rork Max)
  • You're an indie developer or startup with a mobile-first product idea

Choose Bolt if:

  • You're building a web application, dashboard, SaaS tool, or internal admin panel
  • Speed of prototyping is your top priority — you need a live demo in minutes
  • Your audience accesses your product in a browser, not an app store
  • You're building a minimum viable product to validate a web-based idea quickly

Advanced: Using Rork and Bolt Together

Rork and Bolt aren't necessarily competitors — they can complement each other perfectly:

  • Build a back-office admin dashboard in Bolt (web), and ship the user-facing mobile app in Rork
  • Use Bolt to rapidly prototype a REST API mock, then connect it to your Rork mobile app for testing
  • Create a marketing landing page with Bolt while the iOS app is in App Store review

This parallel approach lets you leverage the best of both platforms for different parts of the same product.

FAQ

Q: Can Rork build web apps?

A: Rork is focused on iOS and Android mobile apps. For web dashboards or landing pages alongside a mobile app, pairing Rork with Bolt or a similar web builder is the practical approach.

Q: Can Bolt publish apps to the App Store?

A: No. Bolt generates web applications that run in a browser. To publish to the App Store or Google Play, you need a tool like Rork that generates native or React Native mobile code.

Q: Which tool is better for a complete beginner?

A: Both are beginner-friendly with zero setup required. If your goal is a phone app, start with Rork. If your goal is a website or web service, start with Bolt. Both offer free tiers so you can experiment before committing.

Q: How does code quality compare between the two?

A: Both tools generate production-grade code that an engineer can take over and extend. Rork produces Expo-based React Native or native Swift code; Bolt produces standard Next.js / React code. Neither is perfect, but both are solid foundations for real projects.

Q: What is the difference between Rork and Rork Max?

A: Standard Rork generates cross-platform React Native apps for iOS and Android. Rork Max generates native Swift code and supports the entire Apple ecosystem, including Apple Watch, Apple TV, Vision Pro, and native-only APIs. See our Rork vs Rork Max comparison for a full breakdown.

Summary

| | Rork | Bolt | |---|---|---| | Best for | Mobile apps (iOS & Android) | Web apps & SaaS | | Strength | Native mobile, App Store publishing | Speed, web framework breadth | | Weakness | Not suited for web-only projects | Cannot build mobile apps | | Pricing | Free → $25/month+ | Free → $20/month+ |

The decision comes down to one question: Are you building for a phone or a browser? If it's a phone, use Rork. If it's a browser, use Bolt.

Both tools offer free plans — try them both, then commit to the one that fits your project. To get started with Rork today, check out our Getting Started guide. For a broader comparison of AI app builders in 2026, see our AI App Builders Comparison 2026.

en.title: Building Resilient Offline-First Apps with Rork Max — Local-First Databases, Sync Engines & Conflict Resolution
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Master offline-first architecture with Rork Max. Learn local-first databases, CRDT sync engines, conflict resolution strategies, and production-ready patterns for building apps that work seamlessly online and offline.
en.content:
Building Resilient Offline-First Apps with Rork Max

The future of mobile applications isn't about constant connectivity—it's about working perfectly whether your users have network access or not. Building offline-first apps requires rethinking how you store data, synchronize state, and handle conflicts when users operate independently.

With Rork Max generating native Swift code directly, you can implement sophisticated offline-first architectures that were previously complex and error-prone. This guide covers everything you need to know to build production-grade offline-first applications.

Why Offline-First Matters

Most mobile apps today treat the network as a dependency. Users experience frustrating spinners, failed requests, and lost data when connection drops. Offline-first flips this model: your app works locally first, syncs when connected, and gracefully handles disconnections.

💡
Offline-first apps don't just improve user experience—they reduce server load by batching syncs, improve privacy by processing data locally, and enable use cases like field work, aviation, and international travel where connectivity is unreliable.

The Business Case

  • Reduced latency: Local reads and writes are instant
  • Lower server costs: Fewer API calls through intelligent batching and deduplication
  • Better reliability: App remains functional during outages or poor connectivity
  • Enhanced privacy: Sensitive data processed locally before sync
  • Global reach: Works in regions with patchy or expensive connectivity

Local-First Architecture Patterns

The Layered Approach

Building offline-first requires three coordinated layers:

  1. Local Storage Layer: SQLite or RealmDB for persistent local state
  2. Sync Engine: Handles change tracking, batching, and network communication
  3. Conflict Resolution: Strategies for merging divergent changes

Implementation with Rork Max

Rork Max generates native Swift code, giving you direct access to high-performance local storage options. Here's the foundational architecture:

⚠️
Every offline-first system needs a unique client identifier to track which device made which changes. This is critical for conflict resolution. Use a stable identifier that persists across app reinstalls when possible.

Local-First Databases for Rork Max

SQLite with WCDB (WeChat Database)

SQLite is lightweight and battle-tested, but for offline-first applications, WCDB (WeChat's fork of SQLite) adds critical features:

RealmDB for Complex Relationships

For apps with intricate data relationships, RealmDB excels:

CRDT Sync Engines for Conflict-Free Merging

Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) guarantee that concurrent changes merge correctly without explicit conflict resolution logic. This is the gold standard for offline-first systems.

💡
CRDTs work by assigning each change a unique identifier that includes both timestamp and client ID. When changes arrive out of order, the CRDT algorithm deterministically merges them into the same final state on all devices.

Last-Write-Wins (LWW) CRDT

The simplest CRDT strategy for most applications:

Vector Clock CRDT

For detecting causality between changes:

Conflict Resolution Strategies

Not all conflicts can be resolved automatically. Here are proven strategies for different scenarios:

Semantic Conflict Resolution

User-Driven Conflict Resolution

Sometimes only the user can resolve conflicts:

Implementing a Production Sync Engine

Here's a complete, production-ready sync engine combining all pieces:

Offline-First Testing Strategy

Testing offline-first apps requires simulating network conditions:

Performance Optimization for Offline-First

Indexing and Query Performance

Memory Management for Large Datasets

Common Pitfalls and Solutions

⚠️
**Clock skew issues**: Different devices have different system clocks. For timestamp-based conflict resolution, always use server time when available, and implement clock skew detection to warn users.

Handling Large Files Offline

Handling Deletions in Offline Mode

Conclusion

Building offline-first apps with Rork Max puts powerful tools in your hands. By combining local-first databases like SQLite and RealmDB with CRDT-based sync engines and intelligent conflict resolution, you can create applications that work seamlessly whether users have network connectivity or not.

The key principles to remember:

  1. Always work locally first — This guarantees responsiveness
  2. Track changes meticulously — Version vectors and timestamps enable correct merging
  3. Choose the right CRDT strategy — Last-write-wins works for most cases; fall back to CRDTs for complex data
  4. Test network conditions thoroughly — Use mock services to simulate offline, slow, and flaky connections
  5. Plan for conflict resolution — Implement semantic resolution where possible, but prepare UI for user decisions

With these patterns, you're ready to build offline-first applications that delight users in all network conditions.

en.title: Mastering Deep Linking & Universal Links in Rork Max — Deferred Deep Links, Attribution & Growth
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Implement deep linking, universal links, app links, deferred deep links, and attribution tracking in Rork Max apps. Learn growth hacking patterns, install attribution, and user journey optimization.
en.content:
Mastering Deep Linking & Universal Links in Rork Max

Deep linking is the cornerstone of modern app growth. Without proper deep link implementation, you're leaving untapped potential on the table—users arriving from ads can't reach the right content, referral programs can't track sources, and seamless cross-platform experiences are impossible.

With Rork Max generating native Swift code, you can implement sophisticated deep linking and universal link systems that work flawlessly across web and app. This guide covers everything from basic implementation to advanced growth attribution patterns.

Why Deep Linking Matters for Growth

Deep linking enables:

  • Seamless user experiences: Users land directly on relevant content instead of the app home
  • Accurate attribution: Track which campaign or channel brought each user
  • Higher conversion rates: Users arriving on the right screen close to 5x more likely to convert
  • Referral programs: Built-in mechanisms for viral growth
  • Cross-platform consistency: Same links work on web, in-app browsers, and native apps
💡
Studies show that apps with deep linking see 30% higher user engagement and 4x better retention. More importantly, deep linking enables attribution tracking, which is critical for measuring marketing ROI in the post-IDFA era.

Architecture for Deep Linking in Rork Max Apps

The Three Components

Proper deep link architecture requires coordination between three layers:

  1. URL Scheme Handler: Intercepts deep link URLs
  2. Route Parser & Navigator: Parses URLs and navigates to the right screen
  3. Payload Manager: Passes deep link data to screens

Implementation Strategy

Universal Links — Seamless Web-to-App Deep Linking

Universal Links allow your website URLs to open directly in your app, providing a seamless experience. When a user doesn't have your app, the URL opens in the browser instead.

Setting Up Universal Links

Universal links require two components:

  1. apple-app-site-association file on your domain
  2. Entitlements configuration in your app
⚠️
Universal links are verified using a cryptographic handshake between Apple and your domain. Testing requires: 1. Your domain must be HTTPS 2. The apple-app-site-association file must be publicly accessible at `/.well-known/apple-app-site-association` 3. You must have the proper provisioning profile with associated domains entitlement

During development, disable universal link validation by using xcode-select --install and testing on a real device with App Clips disabled.

Handling Web-Only Content Gracefully

Not all universal links should open in the app. Sometimes you want to keep users on the web experience:

App Links — Android Deep Linking

For Android apps, App Links provide the same verification mechanism as Universal Links:

Deferred Deep Linking — The Ultimate Growth Tool

Deferred deep linking enables deep links to work even if a user hasn't installed your app yet. The user installs the app from the store, and on first launch, gets routed to the intended screen.

💡
Deferred deep linking is transformative for referral programs and viral campaigns. A user can share a link to a specific product, and their friend lands on that exact product after installing—without any server logic in between.

Implementing Deferred Deep Links with Attribution

Launch-Time Deferred Deep Link Handling

Attribution Tracking & Mobile Measurement

Building Attribution Infrastructure

UTM Parameter Parsing

Building Referral Programs with Deep Links

Referral Link Generation

Tracking and Rewarding Referrals

Deep Link Testing & QA

Comprehensive Testing Strategy

Deep Link Debugging Tools

Production Best Practices

⚠️
**Security considerations for deep links:** - Always validate deep link URLs to prevent redirect attacks - Use cryptographic signatures for referral codes - Never expose sensitive data in URL parameters (use encrypted tokens instead) - Rate-limit deferred deep link requests to prevent abuse - Log all deep link navigations for fraud detection

Securing Deep Links

Conclusion

Deep linking and universal links are non-negotiable for modern app growth. With Rork Max generating native Swift code, you can implement sophisticated systems that:

  1. Route users precisely to the content they expect
  2. Track attribution accurately across all marketing channels
  3. Enable viral growth through referral programs with deferred deep links
  4. Provide seamless experiences across web and native app

The investment in proper deep linking infrastructure pays dividends:

  • Higher user engagement and retention
  • Better marketing attribution and ROI tracking
  • Built-in mechanisms for user referrals and growth
  • Cross-platform consistency that delights users

Start with basic deep linking, then layer in universal links, deferred deep linking, and attribution tracking as your app scales. With the examples in this guide, you have everything you need to build growth-focused apps with Rork Max.

en.title: How to Publish Your Rork App to Google Play
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: A step-by-step guide to publishing your Rork-built Android app on Google Play Store—from setting up your developer account to passing review.
en.content:
Publishing Your Rork App to Google Play

Rork generates cross-platform apps using React Native and Expo, which means the same project can target both iOS and Android. Once you've built your app in Rork, publishing it to the Google Play Store is a straightforward process—if you know where to look.

This guide walks you through every step: from creating your Google Play Developer account to passing the review process and going live.

Prerequisites

Google Play Developer Account

You'll need a Google Play Developer account before you can publish anything. Here's what to know:

  • One-time registration fee: $25 USD
  • Sign up at: Google Play Console
  • Use any Google account to register

Note: Unlike the Apple Developer Program ($99/year), the Google Play fee is a one-time payment.

Rork Project Requirements

Before exporting your build, confirm the following settings are in place in your Rork project:

| Setting | Description | |---------|-------------| | App Name | The name displayed on the Play Store | | Package Name | Unique identifier, e.g. com.yourname.appname | | Version | Semantic version like 1.0.0 | | App Icon | 1024×1024px PNG |

Step 1: Generate an Android Build in Rork

From your Rork project dashboard, initiate an Android build:

1. Open your Rork project
2. Click "Deploy" in the top-right corner
3. Select "Android" as the platform
4. Enable "Build for Play Store"
5. Start the build (this may take a few minutes)

Once complete, download your AAB (Android App Bundle) file. Google Play requires AAB format rather than APK for all new submissions—it's more efficient and produces smaller app downloads for users.

Important: Rork Max currently targets Apple platforms only (native Swift/SwiftUI). If you need to publish to Google Play, use the original Rork with React Native.

Step 2: Create Your App in Google Play Console

  1. Go to Google Play Console and sign in
  2. Click "Create app"
  3. Fill in the basic info:
App name: Your app's display name
Default language: English (or your primary market)
App or game: App
Free or paid: Your choice
  1. Accept the developer policies and click "Create app"

Step 3: Set Up Your Store Listing

Before submitting for review, complete your store listing. This is what users will see on the Play Store page.

Required Metadata

Required Assets

| Asset | Dimensions | Quantity | |-------|-----------|----------| | App icon | 512×512px PNG | 1 | | Feature graphic | 1024×500px | 1 | | Phone screenshots | 320–3840px (any ratio) | 2–8 | | Tablet screenshots | Optional | — |

Category Selection

Choose the category that best describes your app (e.g., Tools, Lifestyle, Productivity, Education). This affects discoverability in the Play Store browse experience.

Step 4: Complete App Content Settings

Google requires you to declare the nature of your app's content and data practices.

Content Rating

Navigate to Policy → App content → Content rating and complete the questionnaire. This generates an age-appropriate rating (similar to ESRB or PEGI for games) and is mandatory before submitting.

Data Safety

Navigate to Policy → App content → Data safety and declare what data your app collects:

Examples:
- Does the app collect location? → No (unless your app uses GPS)
- Does it collect email addresses? → Yes (if you have login/auth)
- Is data encrypted in transit? → Yes (Expo apps use HTTPS by default)

Rork apps built with Firebase, Supabase, or similar backends will collect certain user data—check your integrations to make accurate declarations. Inaccurate data safety forms can result in removal.

Step 5: Upload Your AAB and Choose a Release Track

Google Play supports multiple release tracks. Use them strategically:

| Track | Audience | |-------|----------| | Internal testing | Up to 100 testers (team members) | | Closed testing (Alpha) | Invite-only group | | Open testing (Beta) | Any user who opts in | | Production | All Play Store users |

For a first release, we recommend: Internal testing → Open testing → Production.

Uploading the AAB

1. Go to "Release → Production" (or Internal testing)
2. Click "Create new release"
3. Under "App bundles," click "Upload" and select your .aab file
4. Add release notes (e.g., "Initial release")
5. Click "Save → Review release → Rollout to production"

Step 6: Submit for Review and Go Live

After submitting, Google's review team evaluates your app. Here's what to expect:

  • Review time: Typically 1–3 business days (can be up to 1 week for first-time submissions)
  • Notification: Via email and Play Console dashboard
  • Rejection: Address the flagged issues and resubmit

Common Rejection Reasons

1. Screenshots don't match the actual app UI
2. Misleading or exaggerated claims in the description
3. Inaccurate Data Safety declarations
4. Broken links within the app
5. Missing or invalid Privacy Policy URL

Heads up: A valid Privacy Policy URL is required for all apps. If your Rork app doesn't have one yet, host it on a web page and add the URL in Play Console before submitting.

Rork-Specific Tips

Package Name is Permanent

Once your app is published to the Play Store, the package name cannot be changed. Choose something like com.yourname.appname that you're happy with long-term. Set it in Rork before your first build.

Version Code Must Increment

Every time you update your app, the versionCode (an integer) must be higher than the previous release. Check your Expo config:

64-bit Support is Included

Google Play has required 64-bit support since 2019. Rork's Expo-based build system generates 64-bit compatible AABs by default—no extra configuration needed.

Testing on a Real Device

Before submitting, test your AAB on a physical Android device using the bundletool CLI or by sharing your Internal Testing track with a test device. Emulators can miss device-specific issues.

Summary

Publishing a Rork app to Google Play comes down to six steps:

  1. Register a Google Play Developer account ($25 one-time fee)
  2. Generate an AAB build from your Rork project
  3. Create your app in Play Console and fill in store listing info
  4. Declare your content rating and data safety practices
  5. Upload your AAB and choose the right release track
  6. Submit for review and go live

Google Play's lower barrier to entry—a one-time $25 fee vs. Apple's $99/year—makes it a great starting point for indie developers. With Rork handling the cross-platform build, you can ship to both Android and iOS without writing platform-specific code.

en.title: Deep Links in Rork Max — Universal Links and URL Schemes
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Add deep linking to your Rork Max app: Universal Links, URL Schemes, and cross-app navigation with practical implementation examples.
en.content:
What Are Deep Links?

Deep links open a specific screen in your app directly from a URL. Tapping myapp://product/123 launches your app and navigates to the product with ID 123 — without any manual navigation.

Deep links enable:

  • Push notifications that navigate to specific screens
  • Website-to-app handoff
  • Cross-app integration
  • Marketing link tracking

URL Schemes vs. Universal Links

| Method | Format | Notes | |--------|--------|-------| | URL Scheme | myapp://path | Easy to set up. No fallback if app isn't installed. | | Universal Links (iOS) | https://myapp.com/path | Seamless web/app handoff. Apple recommended. | | App Links (Android) | https://myapp.com/path | Android equivalent of Universal Links. |

Configuration in Rork Max

app.json setup

React Navigation Integration

Prompt Rork Max:

"Add deep linking to the app with these routes:
- myapp://home → Home screen
- myapp://product/:id → Product Detail screen
- myapp://profile → Profile screen
Use React Navigation."

Generated linking config:

Push Notification Integration

Include the deep link URL in notification data:

Summary

Deep links are a significant UX upgrade that Rork Max can implement from natural language prompts.

  • Configure scheme and Associated Domains in app.json
  • Define routes with React Navigation's linking config
  • Combine with push notifications for direct screen navigation

en.title: Adding Animations in Rork Max — React Native Animated Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Implement smooth animations in your Rork Max app: fade, slide, spring, and Lottie animations with practical code examples.
en.content:
Why Animations Matter

Animations are what separate a polished app from a generic one. How a button responds to a tap, how smoothly a screen transitions, how the loading state feels — these details define perceived quality.

Rork Max is React Native-based, so Animated API and react-native-reanimated are available.

Basic Fade Animation

Prompt Rork Max to add a fade-in:

"Add a fade-in animation to this component when it mounts.
Use the React Native Animated API."

Generated code example:

Spring Animation

For natural bouncy interactions (button press, card pop):

Slide-In Panel

A bottom sheet that slides up:

Lottie Animations

For complex animations, use Lottie. Prompt Rork Max:

"Add a Lottie animation to the loading screen.
I'll provide a .json animation file from lottiefiles.com."

Place the animation JSON in assets/animations/ and have Rork Max integrate it.

Performance Tips

Always use useNativeDriver: true: Runs animations on the UI thread instead of the JS thread — dramatically smoother performance.

Prevent unnecessary re-renders: Use React.memo to avoid full component re-renders when animation values change.

Summary

Animations in Rork Max:

  • Fade, spring, slide — all handled with the Animated API
  • Rork Max can generate these from natural language prompts
  • useNativeDriver: true is essential for smooth performance
  • Lottie for more complex animated sequences

Thoughtful animations make Rork Max apps feel significantly more polished.

en.title: Adding Offline Support in Rork Max — AsyncStorage and SQLite
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Add offline capabilities to your Rork Max app using AsyncStorage and expo-sqlite for local data storage and sync.
en.content:
Why Offline Support Matters

Modern mobile apps need to work without a network connection — on subways, planes, and in areas with poor signal. Offline capability dramatically improves user experience.

Rork Max apps are React Native-based, so native storage options are fully available.

AsyncStorage — Lightweight Key-Value Storage

For settings, user profiles, and small data, AsyncStorage is the right tool.

expo-sqlite — Full Local Database

For tasks, notes, or offline-readable articles, use expo-sqlite:

Network Status Monitoring

Show offline UI when there's no connection:

Summary

For offline support in Rork Max:

  • AsyncStorage: Settings and simple key-value data
  • expo-sqlite: Structured data like tasks and notes
  • NetInfo: Network status monitoring for offline UI feedback

All three can be added to your Rork Max app via natural language prompts.

en.title: Lovable vs Rork — AI App Builder Comparison 2026
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: A detailed comparison of Lovable and Rork: features, pricing, platforms, and use cases. Find out which one is right for your project.
en.content:
Lovable and Rork — What's the Difference?

In 2026's AI app builder market, Lovable and Rork are two of the most prominent platforms. Both let you build apps using AI with minimal coding, but they target fundamentally different platforms.

Side-by-Side Comparison

| Feature | Lovable | Rork | |---------|---------|------| | Primary platform | Web apps | Mobile apps (iOS/Android) | | Tech stack | React + Supabase | React Native (Expo) | | Target users | Web devs, startups | Mobile app creators | | Monthly pricing | From $20 | From $25 | | Free plan | Yes (limited) | No | | App Store publishing | Not supported | Supported |

Lovable's Strengths

Web-first UI building

Lovable excels at generating React components for web apps. Its native Supabase integration handles everything from database design to frontend in one environment.

GitHub sync

Generated code syncs to GitHub, making it developer-friendly for post-generation customization.

Easy deployment

One-click deployment for web apps, with straightforward custom domain setup.

Rork's Strengths

Native mobile apps

The ability to build and publish iOS and Android native apps is Rork's unique advantage. No other AI app builder makes it this easy to get an app onto the App Store.

Device APIs with Rork Max

Rork Max unlocks camera, GPS, push notifications, HealthKit, and other mobile-native features that web builders simply can't provide.

Fix Now

Rork's proprietary "Fix Now" feature automatically detects bugs and proposes fixes — particularly effective for mobile-specific runtime errors.

Who Should Choose What

Choose Lovable if:

  • You're building a web app or SaaS product
  • You want to customize the generated code yourself
  • You need a full-stack Supabase-backed app
  • You want to start for free

Choose Rork if:

  • You want native iOS/Android apps
  • You want to publish to the App Store / Google Play
  • You need mobile-native features (camera, GPS, notifications)
  • You have no prior mobile development experience

What They Share

  • AI code generation
  • Real-time preview
  • Beginner-friendly interface
  • Ongoing updates and new features

Summary

Lovable and Rork are more complementary than competitive — Lovable for web, Rork for mobile. The right choice comes down to what you're building, not which platform is "better."

en.title: Rork Templates Guide — The Fastest Way to Start Building
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Use Rork's built-in templates to launch your app development without starting from scratch.
en.content:
What Are Rork Templates?

Rork provides a library of app templates — pre-built starting points for common app types. Instead of writing prompts from scratch, choose a template and customize it. Prototypes come together in minutes.

Available Templates

Productivity & Business:

  • To-Do list app
  • Expense tracker
  • Time tracker
  • Notes app

Social & Community:

  • Chat app
  • Social feed
  • Event management

Health & Fitness:

  • Workout tracker
  • Meal logging
  • Habit tracker

E-commerce & Booking:

  • Online shop
  • Booking system
  • Restaurant menu

How to Use Templates

1. Select a template

On the Rork project creation screen, choose "Start from template" and pick one that matches your use case.

2. Enter basic info

Provide your app name and a brief description. Rork generates code based on the selected template.

3. Customize with prompts

Once generated, customize via prompts:

"Change the color theme to blue"
"Add a category filter"
"Add dark mode support"

Why Use Templates

Save time: Basic CRUD operations and navigation structure are already implemented.

Best practices built in: Templates follow Rork's recommended code structure, making them easy to extend.

Pre-built features: Many templates include authentication flows, data persistence, and common UI components.

Templates vs. Starting from Scratch

| Situation | Recommendation | |-----------|----------------| | Common app type | Template | | Unique UI/logic needed | From scratch | | First app project | Template | | Learning purposes | Either |

Summary

Rork templates dramatically lower the barrier to starting app development. For first-time developers or rapid prototyping, they're the best starting point — build something working first, then add your unique touches.

en.title: Rork vs Lovable: Which AI App Builder Should You Choose in 2026?
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Rork and Lovable are both AI app builders, but serve different platforms. Compare features, pricing, and use cases to pick the right tool for your project.
en.content:
Rork vs Lovable: Two Different Visions of AI App Development

The AI app builder market has exploded in 2026, and two names keep coming up in conversations: Rork for mobile apps and Lovable for web applications. Both let you build software with natural language prompts, but they target fundamentally different platforms and use cases.

This guide breaks down exactly what sets them apart — from core capabilities to pricing to ideal use cases — so you can make a confident, informed choice.

At a Glance

Rork

Rork is the go-to AI builder for native mobile apps on iOS and Android. Backed by $2.8 million from Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) and used by over 743,000 monthly visitors, Rork generates React Native (Expo) code from plain-English prompts and guides you all the way to App Store and Google Play submission.

In February 2026, Rork introduced Rork Max — a next-generation mode that generates native Swift code instead of React Native. This means you can build apps for iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, and macOS with a single prompt, all using Apple-native performance.

Lovable

Lovable is built for web applications and SaaS products. It generates React + TypeScript web apps with seamless integrations for Supabase and Stripe, making it a favorite among early-stage startups that need a polished web frontend fast. If your users will access your product through a browser rather than an app store, Lovable is worth exploring.

Feature Comparison
