| Feature | Rork | Lovable | |---------|------|---------| | Primary output | Mobile apps (iOS/Android) | Web apps / SaaS | | Tech stack | React Native (Expo) / Swift | React + TypeScript | | UI generation | Native mobile UI | Web UI | | Backend integrations | Firebase, Supabase | Supabase, various APIs | | Deployment target | App Store / Google Play | Vercel, Netlify, etc. | | Device preview | Real-time via Expo Go | Browser preview | | Apple Watch support | Yes (Rork Max) | No | | SEO-friendly output | No (native app) | Yes (web pages) |

When to Choose Rork

Rork is the clear winner when your goal is a smartphone app. Here's when it shines:

1. You're targeting mobile users on iOS and Android Rork generates code that feels native on a phone — smooth animations, proper touch targets, and platform conventions that users expect.

2. You need device hardware features Push notifications, camera access, HealthKit, GPS, Bluetooth — Rork handles these natively in a way that web-based tools simply cannot match.

3. You want to publish on the App Store or Google Play Rork's entire workflow is designed around mobile app distribution. Rork Max's Swift output aligns closely with Apple's guidelines, improving your chances of a smooth review process.

4. You're an indie developer building a monetized mobile app Rork supports in-app purchases and Stripe integration, making it viable for subscription-based mobile products.

When to Choose Lovable

Lovable makes more sense when your users are sitting at a desk or browsing on a laptop:

1. You're building a SaaS dashboard or admin panel Complex data tables, multi-column layouts, and hover interactions feel natural on the web — Lovable generates polished interfaces for these.

2. SEO matters to your product Since Lovable outputs web pages, search engines can index your content directly. Native mobile apps don't have this advantage.

3. You're building a B2B internal tool Enterprise teams often prefer browser-based tools over installing yet another app. Lovable is well-suited for internal dashboards and workflow apps.

The Power Move: Combine Both

Here's something most comparisons miss — Rork and Lovable aren't always competitors. They can work together:

  • Use Rork to build the consumer-facing iOS/Android app
  • Use Lovable to build the admin dashboard or web portal
  • Connect both to a shared Supabase backend
# Full-stack architecture using both tools

[Rork Mobile App]  ←→  [Supabase]  ←→  [Lovable Web Dashboard]
  iOS / Android           DB + Auth          Admin / Analytics

This approach gives you a complete product — a native mobile experience for end users and a web-based management layer for admins — built at AI speed without a dedicated engineering team.

Pricing Overview (March 2026)

Both tools offer free tiers with usage limits, and paid plans starting around $20/month. Rork Max (Swift native output) is available on Rork's higher-tier plans.

Pricing changes frequently. Check rork.com for the latest information.

Summary: Pick Based on Your Platform

| Your goal | Best choice | |-----------|-------------| | iOS app | Rork (or Rork Max for Swift) | | Android app | Rork | | Web app / SaaS | Lovable | | Mobile + web combo | Rork + Lovable together | | Full Apple ecosystem | Rork Max |

If you're building for smartphones, Rork is unmatched in the AI builder space. If you're shipping a web product, Lovable deserves a serious look. And if your vision spans both platforms, there's no reason you can't use both.

Start with the free tier of whichever fits your platform, run a quick proof of concept, and you'll know within an hour which tool belongs in your workflow.

en.title: Navigation Design with Expo Router and Rork: A Complete Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Master navigation design in Rork apps using Expo Router. Covers tab, stack, drawer, and modal navigation patterns, plus deep linking configuration.
en.content:
Navigation Design with Expo Router and Rork: A Complete Guide

Apps built with Rork use Expo Router — a file-system-based routing library that works similarly to Next.js for the web. Navigation is one of the most critical aspects of mobile UX, and understanding how Expo Router works will make your Rork prompts far more precise and your generated code far more structured.

This guide walks through tab, stack, modal, and authenticated navigation patterns, plus deep linking — all in the context of Rork.

What Is Expo Router?

Expo Router maps your app/ directory structure directly to routes and navigation. Each file becomes a screen; each folder can become a navigation group or layout.

Rork sets up Expo Router automatically, but knowing the file structure helps you write better prompts and understand what the AI is generating on your behalf.

Basic Directory Structure

app/
├── _layout.tsx          ← Root layout (shared config)
├── index.tsx            ← Home screen (/)
├── (tabs)/              ← Tab navigation group
│   ├── _layout.tsx      ← Tab definitions
│   ├── home.tsx         ← Home tab
│   ├── explore.tsx      ← Explore tab
│   └── profile.tsx      ← Profile tab
├── (auth)/              ← Auth group
│   ├── login.tsx
│   └── signup.tsx
└── modal.tsx            ← Full-screen modal

Folders wrapped in parentheses () are route groups — they organize layouts without affecting the URL path.

Tab Navigation

Bottom tab bars are the most common navigation pattern in mobile apps. Tell Rork what tabs you need and it will generate the correct Expo Router structure.

Example Rork prompt:

Create an app with four bottom tabs: Home, Search,
Favorites, and Profile. Each tab should have an
Ionicons icon and a label.

A typical generated (tabs)/_layout.tsx:

Stack Navigation (Push / Pop)

For detail screens, forms, or any flow where users navigate forward and back, use stack navigation nested inside a tab.

Nested Stack Inside a Tab

app/
└── (tabs)/
    ├── _layout.tsx
    └── home/
        ├── index.tsx        ← List screen (/tabs/home)
        └── [id].tsx         ← Detail screen (/tabs/home/123)

Dynamic routes ([id].tsx) accept URL parameters:

Navigating to the Detail Screen

Example Rork prompt:

Build a news app with a list screen and a detail screen.
Tapping a news item navigates to the detail screen using the
article ID as a URL parameter. A back button returns to the list.

Modal Screens

Use modals for settings, confirmations, or any screen that should slide up over the current context.

Opening the modal from anywhere in the app:

Authentication Guard

Redirecting unauthenticated users is a pattern almost every app needs. Here's how to implement it cleanly with Expo Router:

Example Rork prompt:

Build an app where unauthenticated users are redirected to
the login screen. After signing in, redirect to the home tab.
Manage auth state with Zustand.

Deep Linking

Deep links let external sources — push notifications, websites, other apps — open a specific screen in your app.

Configure app.json

Expo Router automatically handles deep link resolution from this config. For example, opening myapp://home/123 routes to app/(tabs)/home/[id].tsx with id = "123".

💡
To configure deep linking with Rork, prompt it with: "Set up deep linking with the scheme myapp, and handle the /item/:id path to open the detail screen." Rork will update both app.json and the relevant route files.

Navigation Best Practices with Rork

These five principles will improve both your prompts and the quality of generated navigation code.

1. Define your navigation structure before prompting

Map out which screens belong in which tab, which screens are modals, and what the auth flow looks like before writing a single prompt. A clear mental model leads to cleaner generated code.

2. Be explicit about transitions in your prompts

Phrases like "navigate to the detail screen," "replace the login screen with home after sign-in," and "show this as a modal" directly translate into the right Expo Router calls.

3. Use route groups to separate concerns

Keep (auth)/ for unauthenticated flows, (tabs)/ for the main app, and (admin)/ for any internal tooling. This structure is clean, scalable, and what Rork naturally generates when prompted clearly.

4. Know when to use push vs. replace

  • router.push() → adds to history (user can go back)
  • router.replace() → replaces history entry (login redirects, onboarding completion)
  • router.back() → goes to the previous screen

5. Centralize header styles in _layout.tsx

Rather than styling headers per-screen, use screenOptions in _layout.tsx to keep consistent header appearance across an entire stack or tab group.

Summary

Expo Router gives Rork apps a clean, scalable navigation foundation. Understanding how file structure maps to routes — and how to communicate navigation intent clearly in your prompts — is the single biggest lever for improving the quality of your generated apps.

Start with tab navigation, add stacks for detail screens, use modals for overlays, and layer in auth guards and deep linking as your app matures. Each of these patterns has a clear, repeatable structure in Expo Router, and Rork handles all of it when given a well-structured prompt.

en.title: Build a Real-Time Chat App with Rork × Supabase
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time chat app with Rork and Supabase. Covers authentication, real-time messaging, and performance optimization.
en.content:
Why Rork + Supabase Is a Powerful Combo

Rork excels at generating frontend apps, but for backend needs — databases, authentication, real-time communication — Supabase is the ideal partner.

Supabase is a PostgreSQL-based BaaS (Backend as a Service) offering real-time databases, authentication, and storage with a generous free tier. Combined with Rork, it's especially well-suited for apps that require real-time features like chat and social networking.

Project Setup

Supabase Configuration

  1. Create a project at Supabase
  2. Create the following tables:
  1. Enable Realtime for the messages table

Rork Configuration

Create a project in Rork and provide these instructions via chat:

Build a real-time chat app connected to Supabase.

Features:
- Email sign-up and login
- Chat room list view
- Real-time messaging within rooms
- User avatars

Supabase URL: [your-url]
Supabase Anon Key: [your-key]

Implementing Key Features

Authentication (Supabase Auth)

Rork auto-generates the authentication flow using Supabase Auth, including sign-up, login, and password reset screens.

For additional auth methods (Google, Apple, etc.), just ask:

Add Google login support.
Google OAuth is already enabled in Supabase auth settings.

Real-Time Messaging

Supabase Realtime-powered instant message delivery is automatically implemented. When a new message is posted, it appears in real time on every user's screen in the same room.

Push Notifications

To add push notifications for new messages, connect Expo Push Notifications:

Send push notifications when new messages arrive.
Use Expo Push Notifications.
Suppress notifications when the user is viewing the room.

Performance Optimization

Infinite Scroll for Messages

For rooms with many messages, loading everything at once hurts performance.

Implement infinite scroll that loads 20 messages at a time.
Scrolling up loads older messages.

Image Messages

To add image sharing, connect Supabase Storage:

Add image messaging support.
Upload images to Supabase Storage and
auto-generate thumbnails.

Row Level Security (RLS)

Always enable RLS on Supabase for security:

Takeaway

Rork × Supabase is one of the fastest ways to build real-time apps. Supabase handles the three pillars of backend — auth, database, and real-time — while Rork auto-generates the UI and app logic. Try it on your next project.

en.title: Implementing User Authentication in Rork — Firebase & Supabase Integration Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to add user authentication to your Rork-built app using Firebase Authentication or Supabase Auth. A complete integration guide for React Native developers.
en.content:
Adding Authentication to Your Rork App

User authentication is a fundamental requirement for nearly every app. Because Rork generates React Native / Expo code, you can seamlessly integrate battle-tested authentication services like Firebase Authentication or Supabase Auth.

In this guide, we'll walk through two approaches to adding auth to a Rork-generated app:

  • Firebase Authentication — Deep Google ecosystem integration, excellent for social login
  • Supabase Auth — Open-source, PostgreSQL-native, developer-friendly API

Authentication Flow Overview

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  App (Rork-generated code)                  │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐ │
│  │LoginScreen│→│ AuthHook │→│HomeScreen│ │
│  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘ │
│                       ↕                      │
│              Firebase / Supabase             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rork projects use Expo Router under the hood. The recommended pattern is to manage auth state in a custom hook, then use a root layout guard to redirect unauthenticated users to the login screen.

Using Firebase Authentication

1. Create a Firebase Project

In the Firebase Console, create a project and enable Email/Password under Authentication → Sign-in method. Download google-services.json (Android) and GoogleService-Info.plist (iOS) for later.

2. Rork Prompt Example

Add email and password authentication to the app.
- Create a login screen and a sign-up screen
- Use Firebase Authentication
- Only authenticated users can access the home screen
- Add a logout button to the profile screen

Rork will generate all the necessary code. You'll only need to swap in your Firebase configuration values.

3. Firebase Configuration

Add a firebase.config.js file to your Rork project:

4. Auth Hook

5. Login Screen

Using Supabase Auth

Supabase offers a simpler setup with excellent free-tier limits and native SQL support.

1. Create a Supabase Project

In the Supabase Dashboard, create a project, then go to Authentication → Providers and enable the Email provider. Note your Project URL and anon key.

2. Rork Prompt Example

Implement email authentication using Supabase.
- Send email verification on sign-up
- Login and logout functionality
- Redirect users based on auth state

3. Supabase Client Setup

Important: Using AsyncStorage as the session storage ensures the user stays logged in after the app restarts.

4. Auth Hook (Supabase)

5. Auth Operations

Route Guard: Redirect Unauthenticated Users

With Expo Router, implement a route guard in _layout.jsx:

Firebase vs Supabase — Which Should You Choose?

| Feature | Firebase Auth | Supabase Auth | |---------|--------------|---------------| | Free tier | 10,000 MAU/month | 50,000 MAU/month | | Social login | ◎ (Google, Apple, Twitter, etc.) | ○ (major providers) | | Database | Firestore (NoSQL) | PostgreSQL (SQL) | | Open source | ✗ (Google proprietary) | ✓ | | Learning curve | Medium | Low | | Rork compatibility | ◎ | ◎ |

For indie developers and MVPs, Supabase is the go-to choice — the free tier is generous and the API is intuitive. If Google/Apple Sign-In is a priority, Firebase is the more mature option.

Bonus: Adding Social Login

You can add social login with a simple prompt addition:

Also add Google Sign-In and Apple Sign-In buttons.
Use expo-auth-session for the implementation.

App Store Requirement: If your app offers any third-party login, Apple requires you to also offer Sign in with Apple. Don't skip this if you plan to ship on iOS.

Summary

  • Rork generates React Native / Expo code, making it fully compatible with both Firebase and Supabase
  • Manage auth state centrally using a custom hook, then enforce it with a root layout guard
  • Choose Firebase for Google ecosystem depth; choose Supabase for open-source SQL flexibility
  • Always subscribe to onAuthStateChanged / onAuthStateChange for real-time auth state tracking

Adding authentication unlocks per-user data management, personalization, and a host of other features that elevate your app from a prototype to a real product. Give it a try in your next Rork project.

en.title: Rork's Auto-Fix Feature 'Fix Now' — A Complete Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Everything you need to know about Rork's Fix Now feature. Learn how AI auto-fixes build errors and runtime bugs with a single click.
en.content:
What Is "Fix Now"?

When building apps in Rork, you'll occasionally run into build errors or runtime bugs. Fix Now is a feature that lets AI automatically fix these issues with a single click.

Even without coding experience or understanding of the error, you can resolve problems with just one button press — that's the core appeal of Fix Now.

Types of Errors Fix Now Handles

Build Errors

Automatically detects and fixes errors that occur during the build process:

  • TypeScript / JavaScript syntax errors
  • Incorrect import paths
  • Type mismatches
  • Dependency version conflicts

Runtime Errors

Handles errors that occur while the app is running:

  • Undefined / null reference errors
  • API response format mismatches
  • Navigation reference errors
  • Infinite render loops

Layout Issues

Fix Now can also address visual problems in some cases:

  • Broken layouts
  • Scroll issues
  • Unresponsive to different screen sizes

How to Use It

Basic Flow

  1. When an error occurs, a red banner appears at the top of the screen
  2. Click the "Fix Now" button in the banner
  3. AI analyzes the error and generates a fix
  4. The fix is applied and the app automatically rebuilds

Adding Context via Chat

When Fix Now alone doesn't solve the issue, providing more details through chat improves fix accuracy:

Fix Now didn't resolve the timer error.
The app crashes when the timer reaches 0.
I think the countdown might be missing
a stop condition when it hits zero.

Running Fix Now Multiple Times

If one attempt doesn't work, you can run Fix Now again. The AI considers previous fix attempts and tries a different approach, often succeeding on the second or third try.

How Fix Now Works

Fix Now operates through this process:

  1. Error log analysis: Parses stack traces and error messages
  2. Code identification: Locates the relevant files and code sections
  3. Fix generation: AI generates the corrected code
  4. Auto-apply and test: Applies the fix and rebuilds to verify

Tips for Effective Use

Fix Errors Immediately

Don't continue development with unresolved errors — problems compound quickly. Run Fix Now as soon as an error appears.

Review What Changed

Fix Now is automatic, but make a habit of checking what was changed. Reviewing the diff in the code editor helps you prevent similar errors in the future.

Combine with Chat

The most effective approach is using Fix Now and chat together. Use Fix Now for the initial fix, then fine-tune through chat for precise adjustments.

When Fix Now Isn't Enough

Some situations are harder for Fix Now to handle on its own:

  • Architecture-level issues requiring fundamental data flow redesign
  • External API specification changes
  • Platform-specific limitation issues

In these cases, describe the problem in detail via chat and ask the AI for a broader solution that may include architectural changes.

Takeaway

Fix Now is a powerful ally for smooth app development in Rork. When you hit an error, don't panic — just press Fix Now first. More often than not, AI will resolve the issue in seconds.

en.title: Build Apple Watch Apps with Rork Max — No Xcode Required
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build native Apple Watch apps using only natural language with Rork Max. From creation to App Store publishing — no Xcode needed.
en.content:
Apple Watch Development Just Got Way Easier

Building an Apple Watch app traditionally requires Xcode, SwiftUI knowledge, and an understanding of watchOS constraints. Rork Max changes this entirely — you can now build watchOS apps using nothing but natural language prompts.

Rork Max generates native Swift apps for iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, Apple TV, and Vision Pro. This article focuses on creating Apple Watch apps.

Prerequisites

  • Rork Max subscription (Pro or higher)
  • Apple Developer Program membership (needed for App Store publishing)
  • An Apple Watch for testing (simulator also works)

Step 1: Create a Project

When creating a new project in Rork Max, select "Apple Watch" as the platform.

Project name: BreathTimer
Platform: Apple Watch
Description: A simple breathing exercise timer.
Guide users through 4-7-8 breathing cycles
(inhale, hold, exhale) with haptic feedback.

From this single prompt, Rork Max auto-generates:

  • Main timer screen (WatchKit UI)
  • Breathing cycle animations
  • Haptic feedback implementation
  • Settings screen (adjust cycle count)

Step 2: Customize

Refine the generated app through chat:

Change the timer color based on breathing phase.
Blue for inhale, purple for hold, green for exhale.
Display remaining time as a circular progress bar.

Rork Max's AI understands watchOS design guidelines and automatically optimizes the UI for the small screen.

Step 3: Add Complications

Add watch face complications using natural language:

Add a watch face complication.
CircularSmall type that shows today's completed
breathing exercise count.

Step 4: Test and Debug

Rork Max includes a built-in preview feature that lets you see your app running on an Apple Watch screen — no physical device needed. If issues are found, the "Fix Now" button can attempt automatic fixes.

Step 5: Publish to the App Store

With Rork Max's 2-click publishing, you can submit to the App Store without opening Xcode.

  1. Click "Publish"
  2. Connect your Apple Developer Account
  3. Enter app metadata (description, screenshots, etc.)
  4. Submit for review

Tips for watchOS Development

Use Haptics Effectively

Haptic feedback is a critical UI element on Apple Watch. Tell Rork Max to "add haptics" and it will implement the appropriate feedback types.

Data Sync with iPhone

If you need to sync data with a companion iPhone app, instruct Rork Max to use the WatchConnectivity framework.

Sync exercise history with the iPhone app.
Use WatchConnectivity to send data recorded on
the Watch to the iPhone app in real time.

Battery Efficiency

Battery life is crucial for watchOS apps. Rork Max generates efficient code by default, but for apps with heavy background processing, explicitly mention "prioritize battery efficiency."

Takeaway

Rork Max has made Apple Watch app development remarkably simple. You don't need deep Swift or watchOS expertise — just an idea and natural language. Give it a try.

en.title: Building AR/LiDAR Apps with Rork Max: A Complete Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build native AR and LiDAR apps with Rork Max. Covers ARKit, RealityKit, scene reconstruction, Vision Pro support, and App Store submission tips.
en.content:
Introduction

Rork Max, launched in February 2026, is a game-changer for iOS developers who want to build augmented reality experiences without writing Swift from scratch. Unlike the original Rork (which targets React Native), Rork Max generates native Swift code for the full Apple ecosystem — iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, Apple TV, Vision Pro, and iMessage.

One capability that's generating serious excitement is AR and LiDAR app development. Traditionally, ARKit and RealityKit required deep Swift expertise. With Rork Max, you can describe your AR experience in plain English and get production-quality Swift code in minutes.

What AR/LiDAR Capabilities Does Rork Max Support?

Rork Max can generate code targeting all major Apple AR frameworks:

  • ARKit — Apple's flagship AR framework: plane detection, object tracking, face tracking, image anchors
  • RealityKit — 3D rendering engine with physics simulation and material support
  • Reality Composer Pro — integration hooks for Apple's AR content creation tool
  • LiDAR Scanner API — precise depth data from the LiDAR sensor on Pro/Max iPhones and iPad Pro

A particularly viral demo: a developer built a Pokémon GO-style AR game using Rork Max in a single afternoon. This highlights just how capable the platform has become for spatial computing.

Prerequisites

Device: iPhone 12 Pro or later (for LiDAR features)
        iPhone 11 or later (for basic ARKit without LiDAR)
macOS: Sonoma 14 or later
Xcode: 16.x (Rork Max compiles on cloud Macs)
Plan: Rork Max ($200/month) or free tier (~5 prompts/week)

Note: LiDAR is available on iPhone 12 Pro/Max and later, plus iPad Pro (4th generation and later). Standard ARKit features work on older devices.

Step 1: Crafting Your AR Prompt in Rork Max

The quality of your output depends heavily on how specifically you describe the AR experience. Here are two real-world prompt examples.

Furniture Placement App

Create a native Swift iOS app using ARKit and RealityKit that lets users
place 3D furniture models in their room using augmented reality.

Features:
- Detect horizontal planes (floors and table surfaces)
- Furniture catalog with 5 sample 3D models (chair, sofa, table, lamp, shelf)
- Tap detected plane to place selected furniture
- Pinch to resize, rotation gesture to rotate
- Tap placed furniture to delete it
- Screenshot button to save the AR scene
- Target iOS 17+, iPhone 11 and later

LiDAR Room Scanner

Build a native Swift iOS app that uses the LiDAR scanner to create a
real-time 3D mesh of the surrounding environment.

Features:
- Live 3D mesh visualization using ARKit scene reconstruction
- Toggle between wireframe and solid mesh display
- Measure distances between two tapped points on the mesh
- Export captured mesh as a USDZ file to Files app
- Graceful fallback for non-LiDAR devices using standard plane detection
- Target iPhone 12 Pro and later, iOS 17+

Step 2: Understanding the Generated Swift Code

Here's a typical structure Rork Max generates for an AR app:

Step 3: Leveraging LiDAR for Scene Reconstruction

On LiDAR-equipped devices, you can enable significantly richer AR experiences:

LiDAR Use Cases at a Glance

| Use Case | Accuracy | Frameworks | |---|---|---| | Furniture placement | ±2cm | ARKit + RealityKit | | Room measurement | ±1cm | ARKit + LiDAR Depth API | | 3D scanning & export | High | ARKit + ModelIO | | Game occlusion | Real-time | RealityKit | | Object recognition | High | ARKit + Core ML |

Step 4: Cross-Platform AR — iPhone to Vision Pro

One of Rork Max's most powerful features is generating code that scales across Apple platforms. Here's how the generated code handles Vision Pro:

This platform-aware code generation means your furniture placement app can run natively on both an iPhone (with ARKit) and Vision Pro (with immersive spaces) from a single Rork Max project.

Step 5: App Store Submission — AR-Specific Requirements

AR camera apps require proper permission strings in Info.plist. Rork Max generates these automatically, but verify they're present:

App Store review tips for AR apps:

  • Include a real-device demo video in your screenshots (simulator won't run ARKit)
  • Clearly describe LiDAR requirements in your App Store description
  • Test on the oldest supported device — AR performance varies significantly across hardware

Prompting Tips for Better AR Output

Tip 1: Be specific about device and OS targets

// Too vague
"Make an AR app"

// Much better
"Create a native Swift ARKit app for iPhone 12 Pro and later with LiDAR
mesh scanning. Target iOS 17+. Use RealityKit 4 for rendering."

Tip 2: Request fallback behavior explicitly

Add graceful fallback for non-LiDAR devices: use standard horizontal
plane detection instead of scene reconstruction on iPhone 11 and earlier.

Tip 3: Include performance constraints

Optimize for 60fps AR rendering. Use LOD (Level of Detail) for 3D
models beyond 2 meters. Limit mesh reconstruction updates to 2Hz.

Current Limitations

Rork Max is powerful, but there are real-world constraints to keep in mind:

  • Custom Metal shaders: Complex shader code quality varies — iterative refinement may be needed
  • Large-scale scenes: Apps with many simultaneous anchors may require manual optimization
  • Multi-user AR: ARKit Collaborative Sessions need careful verification post-generation
  • Physical device required: ARKit doesn't run in the simulator — always test on hardware

Summary

Rork Max makes native ARKit and LiDAR app development accessible to anyone who can write a clear prompt. From furniture placement to spatial measurement to AR games, you can build experiences that previously required months of Swift expertise — in an afternoon.

Start with the free tier (about 5 prompts per week) to prototype your concept, then upgrade to the Max plan ($200/month) when you're ready to ship.

en.title: Complete Guide to Integrating Stripe Payments in Your Rork App
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to add Stripe payments to apps built with Rork and Rork Max. Covers PaymentSheet setup, Supabase Edge Functions, RevenueCat subscriptions, and security best practices.
en.content:
Why Add Stripe Payments to Your Rork App?

Building a mobile app with Rork's AI-powered platform is fast — but shipping a revenue-generating product requires a solid payment layer. Integrating Stripe into your Rork-built app unlocks one-time purchases, subscriptions, and marketplace billing, all without leaving the prompt-driven workflow you already love.

This guide walks you through a complete Stripe integration for both the original Rork (React Native) and Rork Max (native Swift), with working code examples you can drop directly into your project.

Why Stripe?

Stripe operates in 180+ countries and supports credit cards, debit cards, Apple Pay, Google Pay, and regional payment methods. For React Native apps — the runtime Rork generates — Stripe provides a first-party SDK (@stripe/stripe-react-native) that offers a pre-built PaymentSheet UI, reducing payment screen implementation to just a few lines of code.

Prerequisites

1. Create a Stripe Account

Sign up at Stripe Dashboard and collect:

  • Publishable Key (safe to expose in client code): pk_live_XXXX or pk_test_XXXX
  • Secret Key (server-only): never embed this in client code

2. Choose a Backend

Stripe requires a server to create PaymentIntents securely. Pair your Rork app with one of the following:

  • Supabase Edge Functions (recommended — works seamlessly with Rork)
  • Firebase Cloud Functions
  • Cloudflare Workers

Prompting Rork for Payment Integration

Rork understands natural language, so use a descriptive prompt to scaffold the integration:

Add Stripe payments to this app.
When the user taps "Buy Now", open a PaymentSheet for $12.00.
Use a Supabase Edge Function as the backend to create the PaymentIntent.
After a successful payment, unlock the premium content screen.

Rork will generate:

  1. Installation of @stripe/stripe-react-native
  2. A StripeProvider wrapper at the app root
  3. PaymentSheet initialization with your Supabase function endpoint
  4. Post-payment state updates to reveal gated content

Code Walkthrough

Client — React Native (Rork-generated)

Server — Supabase Edge Function

Deploy with supabase functions deploy create-payment-intent and add your STRIPE_SECRET_KEY to the Supabase dashboard secrets.

Subscriptions with RevenueCat

For recurring billing, RevenueCat is the easiest path when building with Rork. It wraps both the App Store and Google Play subscription APIs behind a single interface.

Prompt Rork to scaffold RevenueCat:

Add RevenueCat subscriptions.
Users on the free tier see ads.
Subscribers on the $4.99/month plan get an ad-free experience and unlimited saves.

Rork will wire up:

  • RevenueCat SDK initialization
  • Paywall screen with plan selection
  • Purchase and restore-purchase flows
  • Entitlement checks to gate features

RevenueCat advantages over raw StoreKit or Google Play Billing:

| Feature | RevenueCat | Manual Implementation | |---------|-----------|----------------------| | Cross-platform billing | ✅ Unified API | ❌ Two separate SDKs | | Receipt validation | ✅ Automatic | ❌ Custom server logic | | Revenue analytics | ✅ Built-in dashboard | ❌ DIY | | Webhooks (Supabase sync) | ✅ Easy | ❌ Complex |

Payments in Rork Max (Native Swift)

Rork Max generates native SwiftUI apps, so Apple's native payment stack is available out of the box.

StoreKit 2 (recommended for in-app purchases):

Implement StoreKit 2 in-app purchases.
Offer a $9.99 one-time purchase to unlock the full recipe library.
Show a paywall sheet when a free user tries to open a locked recipe.

Stripe iOS SDK for custom payment flows:

Add the Stripe iOS SDK to this SwiftUI app.
When the user checks out, present a Stripe Payment Sheet for $29.00.
Call the Supabase Edge Function to create the PaymentIntent.

Rork Max will generate native Swift classes for the STPPaymentContext or the newer PaymentSheet Swift API.

Security Checklist

Stripe integration introduces sensitive keys — handle them carefully:

| Rule | Implementation | |------|---------------| | Never embed Secret Key in client | Use environment variables on the server | | Use test keys during development | pk_test_ / sk_test_ in dev, pk_live_ / sk_live_ in production | | Validate Webhook signatures | Use STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET to verify event authenticity | | Enable HTTPS everywhere | Supabase Edge Functions are HTTPS by default |

Tell Rork to load secrets from environment variables:

Load STRIPE_SECRET_KEY from environment variables.
Never hardcode it in any file.

Go-Live Checklist

Before submitting to the App Store or Google Play:

  • [ ] Stripe account KYC (identity verification) complete
  • [ ] End-to-end test payment flow with 4242 4242 4242 4242 test card
  • [ ] Webhook endpoints configured for payment_intent.succeeded and payment_intent.payment_failed
  • [ ] Error handling for network failures and declined cards
  • [ ] Apple review: digital goods sold through In-App Purchase (App Store rules)
  • [ ] Privacy policy and Terms of Service include payment terms
  • [ ] RevenueCat or StoreKit receipts validated server-side

Summary

Adding Stripe payments to a Rork app requires three components working together:

  1. A backend function (Supabase Edge Function) that creates PaymentIntents server-side with your Stripe Secret Key.
  2. The Stripe React Native SDK's PaymentSheet on the client, initialized with the PaymentIntent secret from your backend.
  3. RevenueCat for subscriptions, which abstracts the App Store and Google Play billing complexity into a single API.

Rork Max users can take the same prompt-driven approach but leverage native StoreKit 2 and the Stripe iOS SDK for a fully native payment experience. Whichever path you choose, Rork's AI scaffolding means you can go from idea to a working payment screen in under an hour.

en.title: Building a HealthKit App with Rork Max: The Complete Developer Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build a native iOS health tracking app using Rork Max and Apple HealthKit. From step count queries to sleep analysis and Apple Watch support.
en.content:
Combining Rork Max with HealthKit for Native Health Apps

Health and fitness tracking has become one of the most compelling categories in the App Store. With Apple Watch on millions of wrists, users expect apps that tap into real health data—steps, heart rate, sleep quality, calories burned. The challenge has always been that building HealthKit integrations requires deep Xcode and Swift knowledge.

Rork Max changes that equation entirely. Because it generates native SwiftUI code (not React Native bridges), HealthKit integration works exactly as Apple designed it. In this guide, we'll walk through building a complete fitness tracking dashboard using Rork Max—covering permissions, data queries, Charts framework visualization, and Apple Watch support.

Why Rork Max is Built for HealthKit

The original Rork platform generates React Native / Expo apps. HealthKit works on React Native, but requires third-party libraries like react-native-health and loses some fidelity with native APIs.

Rork Max is different: it generates pure Swift and SwiftUI code, compiled on Rork's cloud Mac fleet. HealthKit is a native Apple framework, so there are no compatibility layers, no bridge overhead, and no missing API coverage.

The AI powering Rork Max (Claude Opus) has deep knowledge of:

  • HKHealthStore lifecycle management
  • HKSampleQuery, HKStatisticsQuery, and HKAnchoredObjectQuery
  • Authorization flow patterns
  • Async/await integration with HealthKit callbacks
  • Apple Watch companion app architecture

Prompt Design: Getting the Most Out of Rork Max

The quality of your prompt directly determines the quality of your generated app. Here's an effective prompt template for a HealthKit app:

Build a fitness tracking app in SwiftUI with HealthKit integration.

Features:
- Live step count with today's total
- 7-day step history bar chart using the Charts framework
- Resting heart rate display (latest value)
- Last night's sleep duration
- HealthKit permission request screen with explanations

Design:
- Dark mode compatible
- Ring-style circular progress for step goal (10,000 steps)
- Card-based metrics layout
- Smooth animations on data load
- watchOS 10 companion target

This level of specificity gives Rork Max everything it needs to generate a production-quality implementation.

HealthKit Authorization Setup

Every HealthKit app must request permission before reading health data. Here's the pattern Rork Max generates:

Info.plist Keys

Authorization Manager (SwiftUI + async/await)

Querying Step Count Data

Rork Max consistently generates clean HKStatisticsQuery patterns for cumulative data like steps:

The @MainActor isolation here is a Swift 6 concurrency pattern—Rork Max generates this correctly without any manual prompting.

Weekly Steps with Apple Charts Framework

Visualizing historical data requires querying multiple days and rendering them with the Charts framework. Here's how Rork Max handles it:

Chart View

Main Dashboard with Ring Progress

The circular progress ring—inspired by Apple Watch Activity rings—creates an immediately familiar UI:

Apple Watch Companion App

Rork Max generates watchOS targets alongside iOS. A compact Watch view that shows the same health data:

Key Considerations for App Store Approval

HealthKit apps go through additional App Store review scrutiny. Keep these in mind when building with Rork Max:

Usage descriptions must be specific. Apple rejects vague strings like "We need health access." Be explicit: describe which data types you read and why. Rork Max generally generates good defaults, but review them before submission.

HealthKit availability check is mandatory. Always include HKHealthStore.isHealthDataAvailable() before accessing the store. Rork Max includes this check automatically—never remove it, as HealthKit isn't available on all devices.

Health data stays on device. If you're transmitting any health data to a server, this must be clearly disclosed. Building with Rork Max in a local-only mode (no backend sync) simplifies your privacy story considerably.

Real device testing is essential. iOS Simulator has limited HealthKit support. Budget time to test on actual iPhone and Apple Watch hardware before App Store submission.

Putting It All Together

Rork Max removes the most significant barrier to building HealthKit apps: you no longer need Xcode expertise or deep Swift knowledge to create a production-quality health tracking application. With the right prompt design, Rork Max generates:

  • Complete authorization flow with proper privacy descriptions
  • Efficient HKStatisticsQuery and HKStatisticsCollectionQuery patterns
  • Beautiful Charts framework visualizations
  • Apple Watch companion app
  • Swift 6 concurrency patterns (@MainActor, async/await)

At $200/month for Rork Max (plus $99/year for Apple Developer Program), independent developers can now ship polished, native HealthKit apps that would previously have required months of iOS expertise to build. For anyone serious about entering the health and fitness app space in 2026, this is the fastest path from idea to App Store.

en.title: Complete Guide to Building Home Screen & Lock Screen Widgets with Rork Max
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build iOS Home Screen and Lock Screen widgets using Rork Max — no Xcode knowledge required. Includes real Swift code examples and prompt tips.
en.content:
Introduction

iOS widgets — those glanceable displays on your Home Screen and Lock Screen — are one of the most powerful ways to keep users engaged with your app. But traditionally, building them required deep knowledge of WidgetKit, Apple's native Swift framework, which puts them out of reach for most cross-platform tools like React Native or Expo.

Rork Max changes everything. Because it generates true native Swift code, it has full access to WidgetKit. In this guide, we'll walk through building widgets from scratch using only conversational prompts — no Xcode expertise needed.

Widget Types and Sizes

Rork Max supports all major widget types:

Home Screen Widgets

| Size | Description | |------|-------------| | Small | 2×2 grid | | Medium | 4×2 grid (landscape) | | Large | 4×4 grid | | Extra Large | iPad only |

Lock Screen Widgets (iOS 16+)

| Type | Description | |------|-------------| | Circular | Round badge near the clock | | Rectangular | Horizontal banner below the clock | | Inline | Text displayed above the clock face |

Step 1: Setting Up Your Project

Start by creating a new native iOS project in Rork Max, or add widgets to an existing one.

Recommended Prompt

Build a task management app.
Add a Small Home Screen widget showing today's task count,
and a Circular Lock Screen widget showing the next task name.

Rork Max will automatically generate:

  • The main app target
  • A Widget Extension target (e.g., TaskWidget)
  • AppIntent code for App ↔ Widget data sharing
  • App Groups entitlement configuration for the shared data container

Step 2: Understanding the WidgetKit Structure

Every widget Rork Max generates is built around three components:

Provider (Timeline Data Source)

Entry (Data Model)

Widget View (UI Layer)

Step 3: Sharing Data Between App and Widget

Widgets run in their own separate process, isolated from your main app. The bridge between them is App Groups — a shared UserDefaults container that both targets can read and write.

Rork Max handles the App Groups configuration automatically. Here's what the generated code looks like:

Prompt to trigger this behavior

When a task is marked complete, automatically refresh the widget data.

Rork Max will inject WidgetCenter.shared.reloadAllTimelines() into every task completion action.

Step 4: Designing the Lock Screen Widget

Lock Screen widgets have tight space constraints. Keep them minimal and scannable:

Step 5: User-Configurable Widgets with AppIntent

Starting with iOS 17, you can let users configure widgets using AppIntent. For example, choosing which project's tasks to display.

Prompt

Let users pick which project to display in the widget settings.

Rork Max will generate:

Prompt Tips for Better Widgets

When refining your widgets in Rork Max, be specific about both visuals and behavior:

| Goal | Effective Prompt | |------|-----------------| | Visual style | "Give the widget a gradient background from blue to purple" | | Update frequency | "Refresh the widget every 30 minutes" | | Dark Mode | "Make sure the widget looks great in both Light and Dark Mode" | | Tap behavior | "Tapping the widget should open the Add Task screen" | | Multiple sizes | "Show a list of 3 tasks in the Medium size widget" |

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Widget data isn't updating

Check that the App Groups Bundle ID is consistent across both targets. Use this prompt:

Verify that App Groups are configured correctly and fix the data not refreshing issue.

Widget preview shows placeholder data

The placeholder() method is used in previews and should show representative dummy data. Rork Max generates this automatically, but you can fine-tune it by specifying what sample data to use.

Updates feel delayed

WidgetKit throttles refresh requests. For time-sensitive updates, always call WidgetCenter.shared.reloadAllTimelines() explicitly from your app's code when the underlying data changes.

Summary

With Rork Max, you can ship polished, production-grade iOS widgets without touching Xcode directly or learning WidgetKit from scratch.

  • Full support for Home Screen (Small, Medium, Large) and Lock Screen (Circular, Rectangular, Inline) widgets
  • Automatic App Groups setup for safe, reliable data sharing
  • Configurable widgets via AppIntent, ready for iOS 17+

As a next step, combine widgets with Dynamic Island + Live Activities to build real-time updating experiences that extend beyond the widget surface. We'll cover that in an upcoming article.

en.title: Implementing Push Notifications with Rork Max: A Complete Guide
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to implement push notifications in your Rork Max iOS app. From APNs setup to Cloudflare Workers integration, this step-by-step tutorial covers everything you need.
en.content:
Introduction

Push notifications are one of the most powerful tools for driving user engagement in mobile apps. Rork Max — the AI-powered native Swift app builder — makes it straightforward to implement Apple Push Notification service (APNs) integration through natural language prompts. This guide walks you through the entire process, from configuring your Apple Developer account to sending your first notification from a Cloudflare Workers backend.

How iOS Push Notifications Work

Before diving in, here's the end-to-end flow:

  1. Your app requests notification permission from the user
  2. If granted, iOS registers with APNs and returns a unique device token
  3. Your app sends the device token to your backend server
  4. Your backend uses the token to send push notifications via APNs
  5. APNs delivers the notification to the user's device

Rork Max handles steps 1–2 automatically with SwiftUI code generation. For steps 3–5, we'll use Cloudflare Workers as a lightweight, scalable backend.

Step 1: Configure Apple Developer Settings

You'll need an active Apple Developer Program membership ($99/year) to use APNs.

Generate an APNs Authentication Key

  1. Sign in to the Apple Developer Portal
  2. Navigate to Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles → Keys
  3. Click + to create a new key
  4. Check Apple Push Notifications service (APNs)
  5. Download the .p8 key file and store it securely
⚠️
You can only download the APNs key once. Store the `.p8` file in a secure location immediately after downloading.

Note these values — you'll need them later:

  • Key ID: 10-character identifier for your key
  • Team ID: Your Apple Developer team identifier
  • Bundle ID: Your app's bundle identifier (e.g., com.yourname.appname)

Step 2: Generate SwiftUI Code with Rork Max

Open Rork Max and enter a prompt like this:

Add push notification support to my iOS app.
Requirements:
- Request notification permission on launch
- Retrieve the APNs device token and log it to the console
- Use the UserNotifications framework
- Send the device token via HTTP POST to https://api.myapp.com/register-device

Rork Max will generate the following SwiftUI and AppDelegate code:

Step 3: Build the Backend with Cloudflare Workers

We'll use Cloudflare Workers to handle device token registration and push notification delivery — no server management required.

Cloudflare Workers Environment Variables

Add these secrets to your Worker's settings:

| Variable | Value | |----------|-------| | APNS_KEY | Contents of your .p8 file | | APNS_KEY_ID | Your 10-character key ID | | TEAM_ID | Your Apple Developer team ID | | BUNDLE_ID | Your app's bundle identifier |

Step 4: Testing on a Real Device

ℹ️
Push notifications require a physical device. The iOS Simulator will show the permission dialog but cannot receive APNs notifications.

Testing Workflow

  1. Install your Rork Max app on a real iPhone via QR code
  2. Launch the app and grant notification permission
  3. Copy the APNs token logged to the console
  4. Test by sending a push with curl:

Common Errors and Fixes

| Error | Cause | Fix | |-------|-------|-----| | BadDeviceToken | Invalid or expired token | Re-register on a real device | | InvalidBundleId | Bundle ID mismatch | Check your APNs key settings | | ExpiredProviderToken | JWT older than 1 hour | Implement JWT refresh logic | | TooManyRequests | APNs rate limit hit | Add throttling or batch sends |

Next Steps

You've now built a complete push notification system using Rork Max and Cloudflare Workers. Here are some enhancements to explore — just prompt Rork Max for each:

  • Scheduled notifications: Use local notifications to alert users at specific times
  • Rich notifications: Attach images or videos to your alerts
  • Interactive notifications: Add reply buttons or custom action categories
  • Badge management: Control the red badge count on your app icon

The combination of Rork Max's native Swift generation and Cloudflare Workers' global edge network gives you a production-ready notification system with minimal code — and no backend servers to maintain.

en.title: Rork Max Prompt Engineering — 10 Tips for Building Better Apps with AI
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Master Rork Max prompt engineering with proven tips and examples. Learn how to write effective prompts that produce higher-quality native Swift apps.
en.content:
Rork Max Prompt Engineering — 10 Tips for Building Better Apps

Rork Max turns natural language into native Swift apps, but the quality of your output depends heavily on the quality of your input. A vague prompt produces a generic app; a well-crafted prompt produces something you can actually ship to the App Store.

This guide covers practical prompt engineering techniques specifically for Rork Max, with real examples showing the difference between weak and effective prompts.

Why Prompt Quality Matters

Rork Max is powered by Claude Code and Opus 4.6, which interprets your instructions and generates SwiftUI code. The AI makes countless decisions during generation — navigation structure, data models, color schemes, animations, error handling — and your prompt is the only way to guide those decisions.

Better prompts lead to fewer revision cycles, cleaner code, and apps that match your vision from the first generation.

Tip 1: Describe the Complete User Experience

Instead of listing features, describe what the user sees and does from the moment they open the app.

Weak Prompt

Build a workout tracker app with exercises, sets, and reps.

Strong Prompt

Build a workout tracker app. When the user opens the app, they see
today's workout plan with exercises listed as cards. Tapping a card
expands it to show sets and reps with checkboxes. A floating "+"
button lets them add custom exercises. After completing all sets,
a congratulations animation plays and the workout summary is saved
to a history screen accessible from a bottom tab.

The strong prompt gives the AI a clear mental model of the user flow, which produces a much more coherent and polished result.

Tip 2: Specify the Visual Style Early

Rork Max generates both logic and UI. If you don't specify a visual direction, you get a default look that may not match your brand.

Example

Use a dark theme with deep navy (#1A1B2E) as the primary background.
Accent colors should be electric blue (#4A90D9) for interactive
elements and soft white (#F0F0F5) for text. Use SF Pro Rounded for
headings and SF Pro Text for body copy. Cards should have subtle
glass-morphism effects with rounded corners (16pt radius).

Include hex colors, font preferences, corner radius values, and visual effects. The more specific you are about design, the closer the output matches your vision.

Tip 3: Define the Navigation Structure

Don't let the AI guess how screens connect. Explicitly state the navigation pattern.

Example

Use a tab bar with 4 tabs: Home (house icon), Search (magnifying glass),
Favorites (heart), and Profile (person). The Home tab uses a
NavigationStack for drill-down into detail views. The Search tab has
a search bar at the top with filter chips below it.

Specifying the navigation pattern avoids one of the most common issues — confusing or inconsistent screen transitions.

Tip 4: Include Edge Cases and Empty States

Real apps need to handle situations where there's no data, no network connection, or unexpected input.

Example

When the recipe list is empty, show a friendly illustration with
the text "No recipes yet — tap + to add your first one." If the
network request fails, show a retry button with the message
"Couldn't load recipes. Check your connection and try again."
Loading states should use skeleton views that match the card layout.

Edge cases are where apps feel polished or broken. Mentioning them in your prompt ensures the AI generates proper handling.

Tip 5: Break Complex Apps into Iterative Prompts

Rork Max supports an iterative development workflow. For complex apps, start with the core functionality and refine in follow-up prompts.

Iteration Strategy

  1. First prompt: Core screens and navigation structure
  2. Second prompt: Data persistence and state management
  3. Third prompt: Animations, transitions, and polish
  4. Fourth prompt: Native API integrations (HealthKit, ARKit, etc.)

Each iteration builds on the previous result. This approach is more reliable than trying to describe everything in a single massive prompt.

Tip 6: Reference Apple Platform Features by Name

Rork Max generates native Swift, so you can directly reference Apple frameworks and features. The AI understands these terms precisely.

Effective References

Use SwiftUI's .matchedGeometryEffect for the transition between
the list and detail views. Implement haptic feedback using
UIImpactFeedbackGenerator on button taps. Add a Dynamic Island
Live Activity that shows the timer countdown.

Naming specific APIs removes ambiguity and produces code that uses the correct frameworks rather than generic alternatives.

Tip 7: Specify Data Models and Relationships

When your app manages structured data, describe the models explicitly.

Example

Data model:
- Recipe: title (String), ingredients (array of Ingredient),
  steps (array of String), prepTime (Int, minutes),
  difficulty (enum: easy/medium/hard), isFavorite (Bool)
- Ingredient: name (String), quantity (Double), unit (String)
- MealPlan: date (Date), meals (dictionary mapping
  breakfast/lunch/dinner to Recipe)

This prevents the AI from making assumptions about your data structure that could cause problems later.

Tip 8: Use Comparative References

If you've seen a specific app or UI pattern you like, reference it. The AI has broad knowledge of popular app designs.

Example

Design the habit tracker with a streak calendar similar to GitHub's
contribution graph — green squares for completed days, gray for missed,
and a subtle gradient showing intensity. The weekly view should
resemble Apple Health's ring-style progress indicators.

Comparative references communicate complex design ideas quickly and precisely.

Tip 9: Specify Animation and Motion Design

Animations make the difference between a functional prototype and a polished app. Don't leave them to chance.

Example

When a task is completed, the checkbox should fill with a spring
animation (0.4s duration, slight bounce). The card should then
smoothly move to the "Completed" section with a 0.3s ease-in-out
transition. Use .sensoryFeedback(.success) when the task completes.

SwiftUI has excellent animation support, and Rork Max can implement sophisticated motion design when you describe it clearly.

Tip 10: Test on Device Early and Often

Rork Max lets you scan a QR code to install your app directly on your iPhone. Take advantage of this rapid feedback loop.

After each generation cycle, install on your device and test the actual feel — touch targets, scroll performance, animation smoothness, and text readability. Things that look fine in the simulator can feel different on a real device.

Use your observations to write precise follow-up prompts: "The bottom tab bar icons are too small — increase the tap target to at least 44x44 points" is much more effective than "make the UI better."

Bonus: Prompt Template

Here's a template you can adapt for any Rork Max project:

Build a [app type] app for [target audience].

## Core Features
[Describe 3-5 main features with user flow details]

## Visual Design
[Color palette, typography, component styles]

## Navigation
[Tab structure or navigation pattern]

## Data
[Key data models and relationships]

## Native APIs
[Apple frameworks to integrate: HealthKit, ARKit, etc.]

## Edge Cases
[Empty states, error handling, loading states]

Conclusion

Effective prompt engineering with Rork Max is about being specific without being overwhelming. Focus on describing the experience you want — what users see, how things move, what happens when something goes wrong — and let the AI handle the implementation details.

Start with a solid first prompt using the tips above, then iterate. Each generation cycle teaches you what the AI interprets well and where it needs more guidance. With practice, you'll develop an intuition for writing prompts that produce shippable apps on the first try.

en.title: Building Your First Todo App with Rork: A Step-by-Step Tutorial
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to build a fully functional Todo app with Rork. This beginner-friendly tutorial walks you through project creation, UI design, CRUD functionality, and deployment.
en.content:
Building Your First Todo App with Rork: A Step-by-Step Tutorial

Learning to build mobile applications doesn't have to be overwhelming. With Rork, you can create a fully functional Todo app in minutes without needing extensive coding experience. This tutorial will guide you through every step of the process.

What You'll Build

By the end of this tutorial, you'll have a complete Todo application that allows you to:

  • Create new tasks
  • Mark tasks as complete
  • Delete tasks
  • Persist data across sessions
  • Deploy to mobile platforms

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Rork Max installed on your system
  • A text editor or IDE
  • Basic familiarity with mobile app concepts
  • 30-60 minutes of your time

Step 1: Initialize Your Rork Project

Let's start by creating a new Rork project. Open your terminal and run:

Rork will scaffold a new project with all the essential files. Your project structure will include:

  • /src - Where your app code lives
  • rork.config.js - Project configuration
  • package.json - Dependencies

Next, install the required dependencies:

This installs React Native, Rork components, and other necessary libraries.

Step 2: Design Your UI with Rork Prompts

One of Rork's superpowers is generating UI components through natural language prompts. Let's create a beautiful todo app interface by telling Rork exactly what we want.

Open the Rork UI builder and use this prompt:

Create a clean todo app interface with:
- A header showing "My Tasks"
- An input field to add new tasks
- A submit button labeled "Add Task"
- A list displaying all tasks
- Each task should have a checkbox to mark complete
- Each task should have a delete button (trash icon)
- Use a light blue color scheme with rounded corners
- Add some padding and spacing for a modern look

Rork will generate a beautiful, responsive UI component automatically. You can preview it instantly and make adjustments using follow-up prompts:

Make the task items have a subtle shadow and add a smooth animation when tasks are added

The generated component will be placed in your /src/components directory.

Step 3: Add CRUD Functionality

Now let's make your app functional. You'll handle four essential operations:

Create (Add Tasks)

First, set up state management. Ask Rork to create a task service:

Create a task management service that:
- Stores tasks in an array with unique IDs
- Each task has: id, title, completed status, and timestamp
- Has functions to add, get, update, and delete tasks
- Uses AsyncStorage for persistence
- Exports functions: addTask, getAllTasks, updateTask, deleteTask

Read (Display Tasks)

Rork will generate a component that displays your tasks. Use this prompt:

Create a task list component that:
- Fetches all tasks when it mounts
- Displays tasks in order (incomplete first, then complete)
- Shows the number of remaining tasks
- Refreshes when tasks are updated
- Handles empty state with a friendly message

Update (Mark Complete)

Handle task completion:

Create a function that:
- Toggles a task's completed status
- Updates the visual appearance (strikethrough text for completed tasks)
- Updates the data in AsyncStorage
- Animates the transition smoothly

Delete (Remove Tasks)

Finally, add the ability to delete tasks:

Create a delete function that:
- Removes a task by ID
- Shows a confirmation dialog before deletion
- Updates the task list immediately
- Removes the task from AsyncStorage

Step 4: Style Your Application

Let's make your todo app visually appealing. Use Rork's styling prompt:

Apply professional styling to the todo app:
- Use a gradient background from light blue to white
- Color completed tasks with a subtle gray tone
- Add a floating action button for adding tasks
- Use clear typography hierarchy
- Make buttons have hover effects and smooth transitions
- Ensure everything is responsive and works on different screen sizes

You can adjust colors and styling by providing feedback:

Change the primary blue to a teal color and make the task boxes slightly larger

Step 5: Test Your Application

Before deployment, let's ensure everything works correctly. Run:

This starts the development server. Test each feature:

  1. Add a task - Type something and press "Add Task"
  2. Complete a task - Click the checkbox next to a task
  3. Delete a task - Click the delete button
  4. Reload the app - Verify tasks persist (thanks to AsyncStorage)
  5. Try empty state - Delete all tasks and check the UI

Use Rork's testing feature by asking:

Create unit tests for the todo app that verify:
- Adding a task increases the task count
- Completing a task updates its status
- Deleting a task removes it from the list
- Tasks persist after app restart

Step 6: Deploy Your App

Once you're happy with your todo app, it's time to deploy. Rork supports multiple platforms.

For iOS:

For Android:

Ask Rork for guidance on your target platform:

Walk me through deploying this todo app to iOS TestFlight so I can share it with beta testers

Rork will provide platform-specific instructions and handle the complex build configuration automatically.

Tips for Success

  • Start simple - Add features incrementally rather than all at once
  • Use descriptive prompts - The more detail you provide to Rork, the better the results
  • Test frequently - Don't wait until the end to test functionality
  • Ask for help - Rork can debug issues and optimize performance
  • Iterate - Use feedback to refine your app design and functionality

Next Steps

Congratulations! You've built your first mobile app with Rork. Now that you understand the basics, you can:

  • Add categories to organize tasks better
  • Implement due dates and reminders
  • Create priority levels for tasks
  • Add collaboration features so users can share task lists
  • Build dark mode support
  • Add notifications for task reminders

The beauty of Rork is that each enhancement follows the same pattern: describe what you want, Rork builds it, you test it.

Conclusion

Building a Todo app with Rork demonstrates the power of AI-assisted development. What might take weeks with traditional development took only hours. You've learned how to:

  • Create and configure a Rork project
  • Design UIs using natural language
  • Implement CRUD operations
  • Style applications professionally
  • Test and deploy to mobile platforms

Rork makes mobile app development accessible to everyone, regardless of coding experience. Your todo app is now running on real devices and ready to use!

Happy building with Rork!

en.title: Rork + Figma Integration Guide: From Design to Prototype
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Learn how to seamlessly integrate Figma designs into Rork prototypes. Build efficient design systems and accelerate your UI development workflow.
en.content:
The Power of Rork and Figma Together

Combining Figma's intuitive design capabilities with Rork's rapid prototyping framework creates a powerful workflow that bridges the design-to-development gap. This guide walks you through practical strategies for leveraging both tools to streamline your product development process.

Whether you're building a small MVP or a complex design system, understanding how to sync Figma designs with Rork components can save significant time and reduce design-implementation discrepancies.

Setting Up Your Figma Project for Rork

Success starts with thoughtful preparation in Figma. Structure your design files with Rork implementation in mind.

Creating a Component Library

Use Figma's component system to establish a reusable UI kit that maps directly to your Rork components:

  • Consistent naming conventions: Use hierarchical naming like Button/Primary, TextField/Error, Modal/Confirmation
  • Variant definitions: Clearly define all states, sizes, and visual variations
  • 8px grid system: Maintain alignment across your design system for consistency
  • Property documentation: Add detailed descriptions for each component's purpose and usage

Organizing Your Design Files

Structure your Figma workspace logically:

Project Structure:
├── Design System
│   ├── Foundations (Colors, Typography, Spacing)
│   ├── Components
│   │   ├── Buttons (all variants)
│   │   ├── Form Inputs
│   │   ├── Cards & Containers
│   │   └── Navigation
│   └── Styles & Guidelines
├── Feature Designs
│   ├── Dashboard
│   ├── User Profile
│   └── Checkout Flow
└── Prototypes

Implementing Components in Rork

Once your Figma library is established, translate those designs into Rork components using configuration-driven development.

Component Configuration

Define your Rork components to mirror Figma's structure and variants:

Design Tokens Management

Centralize your design decisions using token systems that both Figma and Rork can reference:

Building Interactive Prototypes

Leverage both Figma's interaction design and Rork's interactive capabilities to create high-fidelity prototypes.

Mapping Figma Interactions to Rork

Translate your Figma prototype flows into working Rork implementations:

  • Page transitions: Document Figma's navigation patterns and implement them in Rork's routing
  • Micro-interactions: Button hover states, loading animations, focus indicators
  • User feedback: Toast notifications, modal dialogs, error states
  • Animations: CSS transitions and Rork's animation utilities for smooth visual feedback

Building a Dashboard Component in Rork

Here's a practical example of composing Figma-designed components in Rork:

Maintaining Design-Code Synchronization

Keep your Figma designs and Rork implementation aligned through systematic processes.

Workflow Best Practices

  • Version control: Track component changes in both Figma (using version history) and code (using git)
  • Documentation: Maintain a living component library doc that links Figma specs to Rork implementations
  • Regular reviews: Schedule bi-weekly syncs between designers and developers to catch divergences
  • Change logs: Document design system updates and communicate them to the team

Responsive Design Strategy

Ensure your Rork components adapt across all screen sizes by defining breakpoints in your configuration:

Advanced Patterns: Using Rork Max

Rork Max provides additional capabilities for complex design systems. Leverage these features for sophisticated prototype work:

  • Dynamic theming: Create theme variants that apply across your entire component library
  • Conditional rendering: Build smart components that adapt based on props and data
  • Performance optimization: Use lazy loading for component-heavy prototypes
  • Accessibility: Ensure all components meet WCAG standards from the design phase

Common Challenges and Solutions

Design Inconsistencies

Problem: Designers and developers create components differently Solution: Create a shared component specification document that both teams reference

Asset Management

Problem: Handling numerous SVG icons and image assets from Figma Solution: Use Figma's export feature with consistent naming, then organize exports in your Rork project's assets directory

Iteration Speed

Problem: Design changes require manual updates in code Solution: Implement automated import processes for design tokens and use Rork's hot reload feature during development

Conclusion

Integrating Figma and Rork creates a seamless bridge between design and development. By establishing clear processes, maintaining component libraries, and leveraging both tools' strengths, you can significantly accelerate your prototyping and development cycles.

The investment in setting up this workflow—thoughtful component architecture, token management, and team coordination—pays dividends as your design system grows. Start with a core set of components, test the workflow, then expand as you build confidence in the process.

With Rork and Figma working in concert, your team can iterate faster, maintain design consistency, and ultimately deliver better user experiences.

en.title: Rork vs FlutterFlow: A Comprehensive Comparison of AI-Powered No-Code Platforms
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Compare Rork and FlutterFlow across AI capabilities, pricing, learning curve, and more. Find the right no-code platform for your app development needs.
en.content:
Introduction

The no-code development landscape has exploded in recent years, with Rork and FlutterFlow emerging as two of the most compelling options for building modern applications. Both platforms promise to democratize app development, but they take fundamentally different approaches. This comprehensive guide breaks down the key differences to help you choose the right platform for your needs.

AI Capabilities: The Core Difference

Rork's AI-Native Architecture

Rork was built from the ground up with artificial intelligence at its core. Rather than adding AI as an afterthought, Rork Max makes AI the central engine of app creation. Users can describe their application requirements in natural language—"Create a task management app with user authentication and real-time updates"—and the platform generates a complete, functional application structure.

Key AI Features:

  • Natural language app specification
  • Intelligent code generation from descriptions
  • Real-time preview and iteration
  • Smart UI layout recommendations
  • Automatic component suggestions

FlutterFlow's Pragmatic Approach

FlutterFlow prioritizes visual design and component-based development. AI integration exists, but it's supplementary rather than foundational. The platform excels at providing developers with a familiar visual interface to build Flutter applications.

Current AI Implementation:

  • Template-based AI suggestions
  • Limited code generation assistance
  • Firebase integration automation
  • Component-level AI refinements

Feature Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Rork | FlutterFlow | |---------|------|------------| | AI-Driven Design | ✅ Native | △ Supplemental | | Natural Language Input | ✅ Rork Max | ❌ No | | Visual Builder | ✅ Available | ✅ Primary Focus | | Template Library | ✅ AI-Generated | ✅ Extensive | | Real-Time Preview | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Code Output | ✅ Optimized | ✅ Flutter-Specific | | API Integration | ✅ Automated | ✅ Manual Setup | | Multi-Platform Deployment | ✅ iOS/Android/Web | ✅ Flutter Target Platforms |

Pricing Models Compared

Rork's Transparent Pricing

Rork offers a straightforward, tiered pricing structure designed to scale with your business:

  • Free Tier: Prototyping and basic projects, perfect for getting started
  • Rork Pro: Team collaboration and mid-scale applications
  • Rork Max: Full AI capabilities, advanced automation, and priority support

The free tier provides genuine value, allowing complete beginners to build functional prototypes without financial commitment.

FlutterFlow's Enterprise Focus

FlutterFlow's pricing emphasizes team collaboration and enterprise features:

  • Free Plan: Basic visual editor with limited features
  • Pro Plan: Team features and higher usage limits
  • Teams Plan: Dedicated support for larger organizations

Pricing Reality: Rork generally offers better value for startups and individual developers, particularly when leveraging AI features. FlutterFlow excels for teams already invested in Flutter expertise.

Learning Curve and Accessibility

Rork: Dramatically Lower Barrier to Entry

The most significant advantage Rork offers beginners is its AI-native approach. You don't need to understand UI frameworks, component hierarchies, or design patterns. Instead:

  • Describe what you want to build
  • The AI generates the application structure
  • Refine through natural conversation
  • Deploy directly

Beginner-Friendly Benefits:

  • Minimal programming knowledge required
  • AI guidance compensates for design inexperience
  • Faster time to first functional app
  • Intuitive error messages and suggestions

FlutterFlow: Steeper but Familiar Learning Path

If you have Flutter or web development experience, FlutterFlow feels natural. For complete beginners:

  • Visual builder concepts need to be learned
  • Component-based thinking required
  • Firebase fundamentals helpful
  • More configuration necessary

Learning Timeline: A beginner might need 2-3 weeks to feel comfortable with FlutterFlow's visual paradigm, while Rork could have them building production apps within days.

Output Quality and Production Readiness

Rork's AI-Generated Code

Rork's AI engine produces remarkably clean, production-ready code:

  • Follows industry best practices
  • Scalable architecture by default
  • Maintainable code structure
  • Security considerations built-in
  • Easy to hand-off to traditional developers

FlutterFlow's Proven Track Record

FlutterFlow generates legitimate Flutter code that runs on production apps worldwide. The trade-off:

  • Code quality depends on design decisions
  • May require optimization for complex scenarios
  • Extensive customization possible through code injection
  • Strong ecosystem support for refinement

Deployment and Platform Support

Rork's Multi-Platform Focus

  • iOS & Android: Native apps for both platforms
  • Web: Responsive web applications
  • Cloud Hosting: Integrated deployment options
  • API Management: Automatic backend configuration
  • Scaling: Built-in scalability for growing applications

FlutterFlow's Flutter Specialization

  • Mobile-First: Optimized for iOS and Android
  • Firebase Integration: Deep, automatic setup
  • App Store Distribution: Direct publishing support
  • Custom Code: Full Dart code access for advanced needs
  • Ecosystem: Leverage entire Flutter package ecosystem

Community and Support Resources

Rork's Growing Ecosystem

As Rork gains popularity, its community is rapidly expanding:

  • Active Discord community with Rork team presence
  • Regular updates and new feature announcements
  • Focus on AI-assisted development patterns
  • Emerging ecosystem of templates and plugins

FlutterFlow's Established Community

FlutterFlow benefits from years of community development:

  • Extensive YouTube tutorials and courses
  • Large Stack Overflow presence
  • Third-party plugin ecosystem
  • Integration with Flutter's broader community

Detailed Comparison: Use Cases

Choose Rork When:

  • Speed is Critical: Launch MVPs in days, not weeks
  • Non-Technical Teams: Business people building their own apps
  • Experimentation: Rapid prototyping and iteration
  • Budget-Conscious: Strong free tier and affordable paid plans
  • Leveraging AI: Making AI part of your development strategy

Choose FlutterFlow When:

  • Deep Flutter Knowledge: Team with existing Flutter expertise
  • Firebase Ecosystem: Heavy reliance on Firebase services
  • Code Customization: Need maximum control and extensibility
  • Proven Stability: Established track record matters
  • Visual Design Preference: Team prefers visual UI building

Performance Considerations

Both platforms handle typical application workloads well. Rork's AI-generated code is optimized by default, while FlutterFlow requires more manual optimization. For small to medium applications (under 10,000 users), performance differences are negligible.

Integration Ecosystem

Rork

  • REST API integration
  • Webhook support
  • Third-party service connectors
  • Growing integration marketplace

FlutterFlow

  • Firebase (deep integration)
  • REST/GraphQL APIs
  • Payment processors
  • Cloud functions support

The Verdict

Rork represents the future of app development: AI-driven, accessible to non-technical users, and remarkably fast. If you're tired of clicking through hundreds of menu options, Rork's natural language approach feels like magic.

FlutterFlow remains valuable: If you need maximum control, have Flutter experience, or require specific Flutter ecosystem integrations, FlutterFlow excels.

The practical recommendation: Start with Rork's free tier for your next project. Experience the speed and simplicity firsthand. If you find yourself wanting lower-level control or already have Flutter expertise, FlutterFlow might be your ideal complement.

The future of app development isn't about choosing between traditional code and no-code—it's about choosing the right tool for your specific goals, timeline, and team composition. Both platforms represent genuine progress in democratizing software creation.

en.title: 10 Rork Use Cases: From Portfolio Apps to E-Commerce
en.author: Rork Lab
en.description: Explore 10 practical Rork use cases from portfolio apps to e-commerce platforms. Real-world applications for developers.
en.content:
10 Rork Use Cases: From Portfolio Apps to E-Commerce

Every successful app starts with a clear purpose: solving a specific problem for a specific audience. Rork excels at rapidly transforming these purposes into polished, functional applications. Whether you're building your first portfolio project or launching a full-featured marketplace, Rork adapts to your needs.

This guide showcases 10 real-world use cases that demonstrate Rork's versatility. Each is achievable for solo developers and represents actual market opportunities.

1. Portfolio / Personal Brand App

What it is: A dynamic showcase of your work, skills, and accomplishments accessible directly from users' phones.

Key features Rork can generate:

  • Project gallery with descriptions, screenshots, and links
  • About section with biography and credentials
  • Skills categorized by proficiency level
  • Contact forms integrated with email services
  • Social media link aggregation
  • Dark mode and responsive design
  • Analytics dashboard showing app opens and feature clicks
